title: legislation-7944140-attachment-Att 1 - Skagit Project Comprehensive Settlement Agreement
baseline_headline: Comprehensive Settlement Agreement Reached for Skagit River Hydroelectric Project Relicensing
baseline_body: The Skagit Project Comprehensive Settlement Agreement is a detailed agreement outlining the terms and conditions for the relicensing of the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project (FERC Project No. 553). The agreement, effective as of February 2026, aims to settle all issues related to the project's relicensing and provides a framework for the issuance of a new license. The settlement agreement is divided into several sections, including the scope of the agreement, reservation of rights, and commitments related to the submittal of proposed license articles and the issuance of a new license. Key provisions include the requirement for filings and submittals consistent with the settlement agreement, mandatory conditions and prescriptions for fishways and environmental protection, and consultation under the Endangered Species Act (ESA) and Magnuson-Stevens Act. The agreement also addresses the issuance of a new license, requests for stays or extensions, and responsibility for implementation. Additionally, the agreement includes off-license commitments, such as early implementation actions and funding for the Skagit Fish Passage Program. Overall, the settlement agreement provides a comprehensive framework for the relicensing of the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, balancing the needs of various stakeholders and ensuring environmental protection and compliance with regulatory requirements.

title: legislation-7944140-attachment-Att 2 - National Park Service Off-License Agreement
baseline_headline: City of Seattle and National Park Service Reach Off-License Agreement for Skagit River Hydroelectric Project
baseline_body: The City of Seattle and the U.S. National Park Service have entered into an Off-License Agreement (OLA) regarding the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, which is currently operating under an Annual License issued by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC). The agreement is part of a larger settlement process leading to the issuance of a new license for the project, which is expected to replace the 1995 License that expired on April 30, 2025. The Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, located on the Skagit River in Washington State, encompasses federal lands administered by the National Park Service within the North Cascades National Park Service Complex. The OLA is being executed concurrently with a new comprehensive FERC Relicensing Settlement Agreement, which involves multiple signatories, including federal and state agencies, Indian tribes, and local governments. The agreement resolves all issues between the City of Seattle and the National Park Service related to the issuance of a new license for the project, including conditions under Section 4(e) of the Federal Power Act. The OLA outlines the terms and conditions for the operation and maintenance of the project under the new license, which is expected to be issued by FERC. The agreement also acknowledges the incorporation of proposed license articles and management plans, as set forth in the Relicensing Settlement Agreement, without material modification. Overall, the Off-License Agreement represents a significant step towards the long-term operation and management of the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, ensuring the protection of federal lands and resources while allowing for the continued generation of hydroelectric power.

title: legislation-7944140-attachment-Att 3 - United States Forest Service Off-License Agreement
baseline_headline: City of Seattle and US Forest Service Reach Off-License Agreement for Skagit River Hydroelectric Project
baseline_body: The City of Seattle and the US Department of Agriculture Forest Service (USFS) have entered into an Off-License Agreement (OLA) for the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, a federal energy regulatory project (FERC Project No. 553) located in Washington State. The agreement is part of a larger settlement process leading to the issuance of a new license by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) for the continued operation and maintenance of the project. The project, which is currently operating under a 1995 license that expired in 2025, is situated on the Skagit River and encompasses federal lands within the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests. The OLA is being executed concurrently with a new comprehensive FERC Relicensing Settlement Agreement, which involves multiple signatories, including federal and state agencies, Indian tribes, and environmental organizations. The agreement aims to resolve all issues between the City of Seattle and the USFS related to the issuance of a new license, including conditions under Section 4(e) of the Federal Power Act. The parties intend for the new license to incorporate the proposed license articles and management plans without material modification, as set forth in the Relicensing Settlement Agreement. The OLA outlines the terms of the agreement, including general provisions, and is expected to facilitate the issuance of a new license for the project.

title: legislation-7944140-attachment-Att 4 - Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe Off License Agreement
baseline_headline: Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe and City of Seattle Reach Off-License Agreement for Skagit River Hydroelectric Project
baseline_body: The Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe and the City of Seattle have entered into an Off-License Agreement (OLA) regarding the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, which is licensed by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) as Project No. 553. The agreement acknowledges the Tribe's ancestral homeland and unique rights in the Skagit River watershed, which were established by the 1855 Treaty of Point Elliott. The City of Seattle, acting through its City Light department, is the licensee for the project, which is located in Whatcom, Skagit, and Snohomish counties in Washington State. The agreement builds upon a previous settlement agreement dated April 29, 1991, and the current FERC Project License issued on May 16, 1995. The OLA outlines the terms of the agreement, including the release of claims, compensation to the Tribe, and the Tribe's support for a new license. The agreement also establishes a dispute resolution process and includes representations, warranties, and remedies. The OLA is comprised of 13 articles, including definitions, settlement agreement purpose, release of claims, compensation, and termination, among others. The agreement is accompanied by several exhibits, including legal descriptions, properties maps, and resolutions from the City of Seattle and the Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe. Overall, the OLA aims to establish a cooperative relationship between the Tribe and the City of Seattle, recognizing the Tribe's rights and interests in the Skagit River watershed while ensuring the continued operation and maintenance of the hydroelectric project.

title: legislation-7944140-attachment-Att 5 - Upper Skagit Indian Tribe Off License Agreement
baseline_headline: Upper Skagit Indian Tribe and City of Seattle Reach Off-License Agreement for Skagit River Hydroelectric Project
baseline_body: The Upper Skagit Indian Tribe and the City of Seattle have entered into an Off-License Agreement (OLA) regarding the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project, which is licensed by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) as Project No. 553. The agreement aims to settle claims and disputes between the two parties, with the Tribe being a federally recognized Indian Tribe with unique rights and interests in the Skagit River watershed. The Tribe is the successor in interest to certain villages, cultural sites, and aboriginal bands that signed the 1855 Treaty of Point Elliott, which reserved fishing, hunting, and gathering rights in the area. The City of Seattle, acting through its City Light department, is the licensee for the hydroelectric project. The agreement includes provisions for the release of claims, compensation to the Tribe, and support for a new license. It also outlines a dispute resolution process, representations, and warranties, as well as the effective date and termination of the agreement. The agreement includes several attachments, including depictions of properties, descriptions of maintenance and cultural significance, and approvals from the Seattle City Council and the Tribal Council. The OLA is intended to resolve issues and promote cooperation between the Upper Skagit Indian Tribe and the City of Seattle regarding the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project. Key aspects of the agreement include the Tribe's release of claims against the City of Seattle, the City's provision of compensation to the Tribe, and the Tribe's support for a new license for the project. The agreement also establishes a framework for resolving disputes and addressing future issues related to the project. Overall, the Off-License Agreement represents a significant step towards resolving long-standing issues and promoting a positive relationship between the Upper Skagit Indian Tribe and the City of Seattle.

title: legislation-7944140-attachment-Att 6 - Swinomish Indian Tribal Community Off License Agreement
baseline_headline: Swinomish Indian Tribal Community Approves Off License Agreement for Seattle City Light Hydroelectric Dams
baseline_body: The Swinomish Indian Tribal Community has approved a resolution (Resolution No. 2026-02) that authorizes an Off License Agreement between the tribe and Seattle City Light for the federal relicensing of the Seattle City Light hydroelectric dams on the Skagit River. The resolution acknowledges the tribe's status as a federally recognized Indian Tribe, organized under the Indian Reorganization Act of 1934, and its governing structure, which includes the Swinomish Indian Senate. The agreement is part of the federal relicensing process for the hydroelectric dams, and the tribe's approval is a necessary step in the process. The resolution does not provide details on the terms of the agreement, but it indicates that the tribe has exercised its governmental authority to approve the agreement, which is expected to have implications for the management and operation of the dams on the Skagit River.

title: legislation-7944140-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Considers Ordinance for Skagit Hydroelectric Project Relicensing
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance aims to authorize the Mayor or her designee to execute a Comprehensive Settlement Agreement and related agreements for the relicensing of the City of Seattle's Skagit River Hydroelectric Project. The project's 30-year operating license, issued by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) in 1995, expired on April 30, 2025, and the project has been operating under annual licenses since then. After five years of negotiations with state, federal, tribal, and other license partners, the City has developed a comprehensive settlement package that addresses outstanding issues and impediments to relicensing. The settlement package is designed to protect, mitigate, and enhance the resources of the Skagit River Valley while securing a reliable long-term source of renewable and affordable electricity for the City and region. The ordinance will allow the City to file the settlement and amended final license application with FERC, which will initiate the environmental and public review phase of their rulemaking. The acceptance of the license package and its associated financial implications will occur through a separate future ordinance anticipated no earlier than 2030. The legislation will create new obligations totaling approximately $42 million over the next 5 years, and the total project cost for the Skagit Relicensing project is estimated to be around $140.7 million through 2031. The ordinance does not directly fund or modify the budget or the Capital Improvement Program (CIP), but any necessary changes will occur through annual budget development and budget supplemental processes.

title: legislation-7944140-supporting-Summary Att A - Skagit Environmental Justice Excerpt
baseline_headline: Seattle City Light's Skagit Hydroelectric Project Prioritizes Environmental Justice in License Application
baseline_body: The Skagit Environmental Justice Excerpt, attached to Seattle City Light's Final License Application submitted to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) in April 2023, highlights the company's commitment to environmental justice (EJ) in the project's vicinity. The excerpt defines environmental justice as "fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people, regardless of race, color, national origin, income, and education" in the development and enforcement of environmental laws and policies. The project, which includes three generating facilities in Whatcom County and transmission lines crossing through Whatcom, Skagit, and Snohomish counties, relies on U.S. Census Bureau data to assess the EJ implications. The analysis aims to identify potential disproportionate environmental and health effects on minority populations, low-income populations, and indigenous peoples, as well as ensure meaningful involvement of affected community residents in the decision-making process. In accordance with Executive Orders 14008 and 12898, FERC is required to conduct an EJ analysis, and Seattle City Light's application demonstrates its efforts to address potential socioeconomic and sociocultural impacts. Furthermore, FERC has voluntarily adopted an Equity Action Plan, recognizing the need to ensure that all communities can benefit from the clean energy transition. By prioritizing environmental justice, Seattle City Light's Skagit Hydroelectric Project aims to promote fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people in the project's vicinity, while contributing to a cleaner energy future.

title: legislation-7944140-supporting-Central Staff Memo
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council to Discuss Ordinance for Skagit Hydroelectric Project Relicensing
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Parks and City Light Committee is set to discuss Council Bill 121177, which aims to authorize the Mayor to execute a Comprehensive Settlement Agreement and five Off-License Agreements (OLAs) for the Seattle City Light Department's Skagit Hydroelectric Project. The project, which consists of three hydroelectric developments, a transmission line corridor, and recreation facilities, has been operating under annual licenses since its 1995 FERC license expired in April 2025. The Settlement Agreement, signed by 15 parties including federal and state agencies, tribes, and environmental organizations, proposes the City's obligations for protecting and mitigating the project's impact on resources. The OLAs will outline specific terms and conditions for the project's operation. If authorized, the Mayor or her designee will sign the agreements, which will then be filed with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) as part of the relicensing process. The relicensing process is expected to be completed by 2030, with FERC initiating an environmental and public review phase after receiving the amended license application. The ordinance is the result of over seven years of negotiations between Seattle City Light and various stakeholders, and its approval will pave the way for the project's continued operation under a new 50-year license. The memorandum estimates that the financial impacts of the agreement on SCL ratepayers will be outlined in the discussion, providing a comprehensive overview of the project's future operations and environmental mitigation measures.

title: legislation-7944140-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle City Light Seeks Approval for Comprehensive Settlement Agreement for Skagit Hydroelectric Project Relicensing
baseline_body: The Seattle City Light General Manager is seeking authorization to sign a comprehensive settlement agreement for the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) relicensing of the Skagit Hydroelectric Project. The settlement agreement is supported by various parties, including the Swinomish Indian Tribal Community, Washington Department of Ecology, and National Marine Fisheries Service, among others. The Skagit Hydroelectric Project, which includes three dams, three powerhouses, and three reservoirs, generates approximately 20% of City Light's power. The comprehensive settlement agreement takes an ecosystem approach, addressing flood risk management, fish passage, terrestrial and vegetation resources, recreation, and cultural resources. The estimated cost of the new license based on the comprehensive settlement agreement is $4 billion, which includes capital improvements to dams and facilities, fish passage, off-license agreements, and river habitat restoration. The agreement is expected to raise retail rates by approximately 5% over the 2027-2032 timeframe, with an average increase of 0.5% per year. If adopted by FERC, the settlement agreement is expected to provide a new 50-year license, restore the Skagit River watershed ecosystem, support salmon recovery, respect Treaty Tribes' fishing rights, and deliver benefits to impacted communities. The next steps include a signing ceremony at City Hall in April and the filing of an amended license application in May, assuming Council approval.

title: legislation-7944140-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Authorizes Relicensing of Skagit River Hydroelectric Project
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance authorizing the Mayor or her designee to execute a Comprehensive Settlement Agreement, five Off-License Agreements, and other related agreements for the relicensing of the Skagit River Hydroelectric Project before the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC). The project's 1995 FERC license expired on April 30, 2025, and the City Light Department has been engaged in a seven-year integrated licensing process to obtain a new long-term license. The new license will require the implementation of measures to protect fisheries resources, aquatic habitats, and Tribal cultural resources, including a fish passage program and a Historic Properties Management Plan. The ordinance also acknowledges the City's commitment to operating the project in a way that balances Tribal interests, carbon-free power generation, environmental protection, and community interests. The Comprehensive Settlement Agreement and Off-License Agreements have been negotiated with Treaty Tribes, Canadian First Nations, federal and state regulatory bodies, environmental groups, and other parties, and will be filed with FERC as part of the relicensing process. The ordinance aims to ensure the continued operation of the Skagit Project, which provides flood risk management, dam safety, and public benefits, while also protecting the Skagit River watershed and its wildlife, botanical, estuary, and mainstem habitats. The City Council has previously authorized City Light to establish and fund an early action Skagit Habitat Enhancement Program to implement habitat and watershed improvements in the Skagit River watershed. The ordinance is in the best interest of the City and City Light ratepayers, and will enable the City to obtain a new 50-year operating license for the Skagit Project.

title: legislation-7934566-supporting-Central Staff Memo
baseline_headline: Seattle to Repeal Ordinance Allowing Residential Uses in Stadium Transition Area Overlay District
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is set to repeal Ordinance 127191, which permitted limited residential uses within a portion of the Stadium Transition Area Overlay District (STAOD). The repeal is in response to an order from the Growth Management Hearing Board (GMHB) dated November 10, 2025, which found that the City had failed to follow proper procedures related to State Environmental Policy Act (SEPA) review, notification to the Department of Commerce, and public participation. Ordinance 127191 was originally approved by the City Council on March 18, 2025, and amended the development regulations in Chapter 23.74 of the Seattle Municipal Code to allow conditional and ancillary residential uses within the STAOD. However, the Port of Seattle appealed the ordinance, citing both procedural and substantive errors. The GMHB substantiated some of the Port's claims, leading to the City's acknowledgement of the errors and acceptance of the Board's invalidation of the ordinance. As a result, the City must repeal Ordinance 127191 to comply with the GMHB's order. The repeal will be briefed to the Land Use and Sustainability Committee on March 4, 2026. The STAOD was originally established in 2000, and in 2023, the area was primarily rezoned to the Urban Industrial (UI) zone, which prohibits residential use due to concerns about potential impacts to proximate Port facilities. The repeal of Ordinance 127191 will reinstate the prohibition on residential uses within the STAOD.

title: legislation-7952577-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: City Council Ordinance Authorizes Payment of Outstanding Bills and Payroll for March 2-6, 2026
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance aims to appropriate funds to pay certain claims for the week of March 2, 2026, through March 6, 2026, and to ratify and confirm prior acts. The payment of these claims includes all financial payment obligations for bills and payroll paid out of PeopleSoft for the covered period. The ordinance is required by RCW 42.24.180, which mandates that payment of certain claims be authorized by the City Council. Failure to pay these legal obligations could result in greater legal and financial liability for the City. The legislation affects all City departments, as it authorizes the payment of bill and payroll expenses. However, it does not have any perceived implications for the principles of the Race and Social Justice Initiative, climate change, or any specific piece of property. Overall, the ordinance is a routine measure to ensure the timely payment of outstanding bills and payroll, and its passage is necessary to maintain the City's financial obligations.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes $410 Million Library Levy to Enhance Community Resources and Services
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is proposing a seven-year, $410 million library levy to be submitted to voters for approval in August 2026. The levy aims to sustain and expand investments in the Seattle Public Library, focusing on five priority areas: Hours & Access, Collections, Technology & Online Services, Maintenance, and Administration. If approved, the levy would increase regular property taxes by $0.19 per $1,000 in assessed value, with exemptions available for low-income seniors, veterans, and people with disabilities. The proposed levy would support the library's operating hours, collections, technology, programming, and maintenance, as well as undertake a seismic retrofit of one library facility. The investments would build on previous levy efforts, with a focus on expanding programmatic offerings, capital building projects, and enhancing access to library materials and services. The library has seen significant usage, with over 3.5 million in-person visits and 12 million website visits in 2025, and patrons checking out an average of 24 books per minute. The levy's priority areas include sustaining operating hours, expanding collections, enhancing technology and online services, maintaining library facilities, and supporting administrative functions. The proposed levy has received strong community support, with 98% of respondents in the 2025 Library Community Survey stating that the library improves the overall quality of life in the city, and 97% saying it is worth the investment.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Summary Att 1 - 2026 Library Levy Projected Expenditures and Revenues
baseline_headline: 2026 Library Levy Projected to Generate $410 Million in Revenue for Various Library Initiatives
baseline_body: The 2026 Library Levy is projected to generate a total of $410 million in revenue from 2027 to 2033, which will be allocated towards various library initiatives. The projected expenditures for the library levy are categorized into five main areas: Hours & Access, Collections, Technology, Maintenance, and Administration. The largest allocation is for Hours & Access, which is projected to receive $218.4 million over the six-year period, followed by Maintenance with $85.9 million, Collections with $58.5 million, Technology with $38.2 million, and Administration with $9.0 million. The revenue for the library levy is expected to come from the levy legal allocation, estimated property taxes, and investment earnings. The estimated property taxes are projected to generate $408.4 million, while the levy legal allocation is expected to contribute $410.0 million. The library levy is also expected to benefit from an estimated $0.7 million in underspend from the 2019 library levy and $1.0 million in investment earnings. The projected revenues and expenditures are expected to be balanced, with total estimated revenues of $410.0 million matching the total projected expenditures of $410.0 million. The projections assume a 99.6% collection rate for property taxes and an annual investment earnings rate of 3%.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Summary Att 2 - Levy Rate and Annual Cost to Homeowner
baseline_headline: Projected Levy Rates and Annual Costs to Homeowners for 2027-2033
baseline_body: The provided legislative text outlines the projected levy rates and annual costs to homeowners for the period of 2027 to 2033. The projected annual costs range from $313.2 million in 2027 to $424.9 million in 2033, with an average annual growth rate of around 5-6% from 2029 onwards. The millage rate remains constant at $58.6, while the annual levy rate per $1,000 of assessed residential value decreases over the years, from $0.19 in 2027 to $0.14 in 2033. The median assessed residential value increases from $872,022 in 2027 to $1,147,680 in 2033. The average annual cost to the owner of a median-assessed residential value is estimated to be around $163, with the average median assessed residential value being approximately $1,004,600. The ordinance sets a maximum millage rate, providing administrative flexibility in case the actual assessed values differ from the projected values.

title: legislation-7952576-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Seeks Voter Approval for Property Tax Levy to Support Library Services and Facilities
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance that proposes submitting a proposition to voters in an election to be held on August 4, 2026. The proposition seeks authorization for the city to levy regular property taxes in excess of the existing limitation for a period of up to seven years. The primary purpose of this levy is to sustain and expand investments in the Seattle Public Library's operating hours, collections, technology, programming, and maintenance. Specifically, the funds will be used to support various library services, including access to materials, technology, and programs for residents of all ages, with a focus on bridging the digital divide and promoting literacy, education, and community engagement. Additionally, the levy will support a seismic retrofit of one library facility, ensuring the safety and accessibility of the library's physical spaces. The ordinance highlights the library's importance as a community resource, providing free services, educational support, and access to information, as well as its commitment to equity, intellectual freedom, and fiscal responsibility. The proposed levy is part of the library's ten-year Strategic Plan, adopted in 2024, which aims to guide investments in library services and bring people, information, and ideas together to enrich lives and empower the community. If approved, the levy will enable the library to continue providing vital services and resources to Seattle residents, while also addressing the need for facility upgrades and maintenance.

title: legislation-7952575-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Aims to Align City Code with State Law on Immigration Status Inquiries
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance seeks to amend the Seattle Municipal Code (SMC) to align with the Washington State law, specifically the Keep Washington Working Act, regarding inquiries into a person's immigration or citizenship status. Currently, SMC Section 4.18.015 prohibits City employees from making such inquiries, with an exception for Seattle Police Department (SPD) officers under certain circumstances. However, the current exception predates the state law and is inconsistent with it. The ordinance would strike the current text of SMC 4.18.015.B and replace it with new language that mirrors the state law, allowing SPD officers to inquire into immigration or citizenship status only when there is a connection to an investigation into a violation of state or local criminal law. The change aims to clarify the circumstances under which SPD may make such inquiries, reducing confusion for the public and ensuring compliance with state law. The legislation is not expected to have significant financial impacts on the City, and the current SPD policy already appears to be compliant with the proposed changes. The ordinance would primarily impact the Seattle Police Department, changing the allowable circumstances for immigration status inquiries and promoting consistency with state law.

title: legislation-7952575-supporting-Central Staff Memo
baseline_headline: Seattle Considers Immigration Status Inquiries Ordinance to Align with State Law
baseline_body:
The Seattle Public Safety Committee is set to discuss Council Bill 121180, which aims to amend the Seattle Municipal Code (SMC) to change the criteria under which Seattle Police Department (SPD) employees can inquire into a person's immigration or citizenship status. Currently, federal law prohibits state and local governments from restricting the sharing of immigration status information with the Department of Homeland Security, but allows for policies limiting inquiries into status. In 2019, the Washington State Legislature enacted the Keep Washington Working Act (KWW), which prohibits local law enforcement from collecting information about a person's immigration status or place of birth, except in cases where there is a connection to a state or local criminal law investigation. The KWW also restricts the sharing of non-public personal information with federal immigration authorities in non-criminal matters. 

The proposed bill, CB 121180, seeks to align the SMC with state law, specifically RCW 10.93.160(4)(a), which allows local law enforcement to inquire into immigration or citizenship status only when there is a connection to a state or local criminal law investigation. The current City law, established in 2003, allows SPD officers to inquire into immigration status if they have reasonable suspicion that a person has been previously deported, is present in the US again, and is committing or has committed a felony. The proposed changes would replace this multi-part test with the state law's requirement of a connection to a criminal law investigation. The bill's passage would bring the City's policies in line with state law and provide clarity for SPD employees on when they can inquire into a person's immigration status. The Public Safety Committee's discussion on March 24, 2026, will be a key step in the legislative process for this bill.

title: legislation-7952575-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Aligns City Law with State Regulations on Immigration Status Inquiries
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance that updates the Seattle Municipal Code to align with state law regarding inquiries into a person's immigration status by Seattle Police Department personnel. The ordinance amends Section 4.18.015 of the Seattle Municipal Code, which previously barred city employees, including police officers, from inquiring about a person's immigration status except under specific circumstances. The updated ordinance allows Seattle Police Department personnel to inquire into or collect information about an individual's immigration or citizenship status if there is a connection between such information and an investigation into a violation of state or local criminal law. This change brings the city's law into compliance with the Keep Washington Working Act, a state law passed in 2019 that restricts data collection and information sharing regarding civil immigration enforcement. The ordinance aims to increase clarity and consistency in interactions between residents and the Seattle Police Department, while also ensuring that city laws are consistent with state regulations. The updated law does not permit random or routine inquiries into immigration status, but rather allows for such inquiries only when they are relevant to a criminal investigation. The ordinance took effect upon passage and signature by the Mayor, with the City Clerk responsible for filing and authentication.

title: legislation-7952574-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Aims to Protect Surveillance Data from Misuse in Immigration and Reproductive Health Matters
baseline_body: The proposed legislation aims to extend protections to the Seattle Police Department's (SPD) surveillance data collection systems, including Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) and Automated License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems. The ordinance would require a mandatory 60-day pause in data collection if the City or its vendor receives a warrant, subpoena, or court order for Seattle surveillance data in a civil immigration enforcement matter or if the data is released for use in a federal civil immigration enforcement matter. Additionally, the legislation would expand the 60-day pause provision to apply to reproductive health care matters, ensuring that surveillance data is not used to target individuals seeking reproductive health care services. The ordinance would also allow the Mayor to issue an executive order to temporarily resume data collection in cases where it is necessary to gather evidence of potentially unlawful acts during civil immigration or reproductive health care enforcement operations. The financial implications of the legislation are minimal, as the SPD's vendor, Axon, can implement a system-wide temporary pause on the ALPR functionality without additional cost to the City. The ordinance builds upon previous legislation, including Ordinances 127044 and 127297, which authorized the expansion of CCTV and ALPR systems in Seattle. Overall, the proposed legislation aims to protect the privacy and rights of individuals in Seattle by preventing the misuse of surveillance data in immigration and reproductive health matters.

title: legislation-7952574-supporting-Central Staff Memo
baseline_headline: Seattle Legislation Seeks to Protect Surveillance Data from Federal Access
baseline_body: The Public Safety Committee is set to discuss TMP-12288, a bill that aims to specify conditions for a mandatory 60-day pause in data collection for the Seattle Police Department's (SPD) Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) and Automated License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems. The legislation is driven by concerns that SPD's surveillance data could be used by federal officers or out-of-state law enforcement to target individuals for civil immigration enforcement or reproductive health matters. Currently, the SPD has not disclosed its ALPR or CCTV data for these purposes, but the bill seeks to establish a pause in data collection if the City or its vendor receives a warrant, subpoena, or court order for Seattle surveillance data in a federal civil immigration enforcement matter. The proposed changes would extend the existing 60-day pause requirement, which currently only applies to CCTV data, to also include ALPR data. The bill is part of a broader effort by the City of Seattle and the state of Washington to restrict the collection and disclosure of personal information, particularly related to immigration status, and to limit cooperation with federal immigration authorities in non-criminal matters. The legislation is scheduled for discussion on March 10, 2026, and will likely involve consideration of the balance between public safety, individual privacy, and federal authority.

title: legislation-7952574-supporting-Central Staff Memo (3/24/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle Council Bill Aims to Protect Surveillance Data from Federal Access
baseline_body: The Seattle Public Safety Committee is set to discuss and potentially vote on Council Bill 121179, which proposes a 60-day pause in data collection for the Seattle Police Department's (SPD) Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) and Automated License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems. The bill aims to address concerns that SPD's surveillance data could be used by federal officers or out-of-state law enforcement to target individuals for civil immigration enforcement or reproductive health matters. Currently, the SPD has not disclosed its ALPR or CCTV data for these purposes, but the bill seeks to establish a mandatory pause in data collection if the City or its vendor receives a warrant, subpoena, or court order for Seattle surveillance data in a federal civil immigration enforcement matter. The proposed legislation builds upon existing ordinances, including Ordinance 127044, which restricted the use of ALPR data, and Ordinance 127297, which required a 60-day pause in CCTV data collection in certain circumstances. The bill also acknowledges federal immigration law, which prohibits state and local governments from restricting the sharing of information regarding an individual's citizenship or immigration status with federal authorities. The Washington State Legislature and the City of Seattle have enacted legislation to restrict the collection and disclosure of personal information related to immigration status, and this bill aims to further protect individuals' privacy and prevent potential misuse of surveillance data. If passed, the bill would extend the 60-day pause requirement to ALPR data, providing an additional layer of protection for individuals' personal information.

title: legislation-7952574-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Establishes 60-Day Pause in Surveillance Data Collection to Protect Resident Privacy and Rights
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance that mandates a 60-day pause in the collection of surveillance data from Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) and Automated License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems used by the Seattle Police Department. The pause is triggered by specific conditions, including the receipt of a warrant, subpoena, or court order for Seattle surveillance data in a federal civil immigration enforcement matter, or if the data is released for use in such a matter. The ordinance aims to protect the privacy, reproductive rights, gender affirming care, civil rights, and safety of all Seattle residents, particularly vulnerable immigrant communities. The legislation is a response to concerns that surveillance data may be used to support mass-deportation efforts, investigate or prosecute individuals for seeking or providing lawful reproductive health care, or compromise community trust in local law enforcement. The ordinance amends previous ordinances (127044 and 127297) to extend the 60-day pause protection to ALPR data and specify additional triggers for the pause, ensuring that surveillance data collected in Seattle is used solely for legitimate local public safety purposes. By establishing this pause, the City of Seattle reaffirms its commitment to being a welcoming and safe city for refugees, immigrants, and all residents, while maintaining public trust in local law enforcement technologies.

title: legislation-7936737-attachment-Full Text: CB 121173 v1
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Updates Land Use and Zoning Regulations to Implement One Seattle Plan
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance aims to amend the Seattle Municipal Code (SMC) to implement the One Seattle Plan, a comprehensive plan that guides the city's growth and development. The plan focuses on creating more housing, encouraging density near amenities and frequent transit, and preventing displacement. The ordinance would update Chapter 23.32 of the SMC, amending various sections and repealing others to align with the plan's goals. The updates would affect the Official Land Use Map, zoning regulations, and environmental impact statements. The Office of Planning and Community Development (OPCD) has engaged in a public participation process, including scoping periods, public hearings, and open houses, to gather input from residents and community groups. The ordinance is a result of this process, which included the publication of a Draft Environmental Impact Statement, a Draft Comprehensive Plan, and a Final Environmental Impact Statement. The legislation also aims to comply with state requirements, including Chapter 332, Laws of 2023 (House Bill 1110). The updates would increase the potential housing supply in the city, promote housing variety and affordability, and add new areas for growth in neighborhoods across the city. The ordinance would have a significant impact on the city's land use and zoning regulations, shaping the future of Seattle's development and growth.

title: legislation-7936737-attachment-Att 1 - Maps of Specific Rezone Areas
baseline_headline: Seattle Rezoning Maps Released, Outlining Specific Areas for Redevelopment
baseline_body: The provided legislative text appears to be a collection of maps and references related to specific rezone areas in Seattle.
