title: legislation-7936737-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Updates Land Use and Zoning Regulations to Implement Growth Strategy
baseline_body:
The proposed ordinance aims to update the Seattle Municipal Code (SMC) by amending various sections related to land use and zoning. The changes are designed to implement the growth strategy outlined in the One Seattle Plan Comprehensive Plan, which focuses on increasing housing construction, particularly apartments and condominiums, in neighborhood centers, regional and urban centers, and along frequent transit routes. The ordinance introduces several key changes, including updates to floor area ratios (FAR) and height limits in Lowrise zones, the creation of a new Midrise zone, and modifications to the existing Midrise Residential (MR) zone. Additionally, the ordinance clarifies screening requirements for surface parking in certain zones and establishes consistent standards for measuring FAR across all zones.

The ordinance is expected to have indirect financial impacts on the city, including increased tax revenue from construction sales tax, real estate excise tax, and property taxes. The increased construction activity is also likely to generate more permit applications, resulting in higher permit review costs, which will be covered by associated permit fees. The implementation of the ordinance will require updates to zoning maps, GIS layers, and other public materials, with estimated technology update costs of less than $25,000 and language translation costs of $5,000 to $10,000, both of which will be paid by Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections (SDCI) permit fee revenue. Overall, the ordinance aims to support the city's growth strategy by promoting more efficient and sustainable land use practices.

title: legislation-7936737-supporting-Summary Att 1 - One Seattle Plan RET Summary Report
baseline_headline: City of Seattle Updates Comprehensive Plan with Racial Equity Focus
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is updating its Comprehensive Plan, a 20-year roadmap for growth and investment, with a renewed focus on racial equity. The One Seattle Plan aims to create a city where everyone has equal access to opportunities, housing, and resources, regardless of their race or ethnicity. To achieve this goal, the city employed a Racial Equity Toolkit (RET) process, which involved community engagement, analysis, policy development, and evaluation. The RET process included an analysis of the existing Comprehensive Plan, racial equity data and analysis, and an Environmental Impact Statement. The city also conducted extensive public engagement with historically marginalized communities, including focus groups, stakeholder workshops, and online outreach. The result of this process is a revised Comprehensive Plan that includes key changes and additions to promote racial equity, such as a new growth strategy and policies to address displacement, housing affordability, and community engagement. The plan is guided by a Statement of Legislative Intent adopted by the City Council in 2019, which requested a racial equity analysis of the city's growth strategy. The final report from this process provides recommendations for achieving more equitable outcomes in areas such as housing, jobs, and community engagement. The updated Comprehensive Plan will guide the city's actions over the next 20 years and is intended to create a more just and equitable city for all residents.

title: legislation-7936737-supporting-Director's Report
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes Zoning Updates to Address Housing Affordability Crisis
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced new legislation, known as the Centers and Corridors plan, aimed at increasing the supply and diversity of housing to address the city's persistent housing affordability crisis. The plan, which is part of the One Seattle Plan adopted by the City Council in December 2025, seeks to rezone land in newly designated Neighborhood Centers, Urban Centers, and along frequent transit corridors to allow for more apartments and condos to be built. The legislation also updates development standards in Lowrise and Midrise zones, including modifications to height and floor area ratio allowances. The goal of the plan is to improve housing affordability, address homelessness, and foster inclusive, mixed-income neighborhoods by increasing access to transit, shops, and services. Additionally, the plan aims to reduce regional sprawl and greenhouse gas emissions by promoting more compact and walkable communities. The proposed changes are expected to help implement the vision of the One Seattle Plan and address the city's growing population, which is projected to increase substantially over the next twenty years. The legislation was transmitted to the City Council in January 2026, and information on proposed and adopted amendments will be available on the City Clerk's website. Overall, the Centers and Corridors plan is a key step towards addressing Seattle's housing affordability crisis and creating more sustainable and equitable communities.

title: legislation-7936737-supporting-Public Hearing Notice (4/6/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council to Hold Public Hearing on Comprehensive Plan Phase 2, Proposing Zoning Changes to Increase Housing Opportunities
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Select Committee on the Comprehensive Plan is scheduled to hold a public hearing on April 6, 2026, to discuss proposed legislation (CB 121173) that aims to implement zoning changes in various areas of the city. The legislation seeks to increase opportunities for housing development, particularly in areas with frequent transit routes and in newly designated Urban Centers. The proposed changes include: rezoning Neighborhood Centers to allow residential development up to 5-6 stories, allowing mixed-use development in Urban Centers, and increasing height and density limits in Lowrise zones. Additionally, the legislation proposes to create a new Midrise zone (M1) for 6-story projects, simplify Midrise setback standards, and rename the existing Midrise zone to Midrise 2 with increased height limits. The goal of these changes is to encourage the construction of apartments and condominiums in rezoned areas, contributing to the city's growth strategy outlined in the Comprehensive Plan. The public hearing will provide an opportunity for citizens to comment on the proposed legislation, and written comments can also be submitted until April 6, 2026, at 5:00 PM. The proposed legislation and additional information are available on the Office of Planning and Community Development's website, and questions can be directed to the designated staff members.

title: legislation-7958605-attachment-Att 1 - Snohomish County 43rd Ave SE Quit Claim Deed
baseline_headline: City of Seattle Transfers Property to Snohomish County via Quit Claim Deed
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has executed a quit claim deed to transfer ownership of certain parcels of land to Snohomish County. The transferred properties are located in Snohomish County, Washington, and are described by their assessor's tax parcel numbers and short legal descriptions. The transfer is part of a project (Project No. RC1592, UPI# 16-0040) related to 43rd Ave SE, specifically the section from SR 524 to Sunset Rd at 180th St SE. The City of Seattle conveys its right, title, and interest in the properties to Snohomish County for a consideration of $10.00 and other valuable considerations. However, the City of Seattle reserves an aerial easement for the purpose of accessing and maintaining its electric transmission lines. The transfer is subject to existing easements, covenants, restrictions, and conditions, and the City of Seattle makes no warranties regarding the title or condition of the properties. Additionally, the transfer includes a covenant regarding environmental conditions, where Snohomish County acknowledges that it assumes all responsibility for investigating, removing, or remediating any environmental contamination, including hazardous substances, and releases and indemnifies the City of Seattle from any claims or liabilities related to the property's condition. The obligation to address environmental concerns is intended to run with the land, binding Snohomish County and its successors, and inuring to the benefit of the City of Seattle and its successors.

title: legislation-7958605-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Considers Ordinance to Sell City Light Property to Snohomish County for Road Widening Project
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council is considering an ordinance that would allow the City Light Department to sell a portion of its real property to Snohomish County for road purposes. The property, located adjacent to 43rd Avenue SE in Snohomish County, is part of City Light's transmission corridor and is needed by the county to widen the road. The sale would include the surface rights of the property, while City Light would retain the aerial rights to continue operating and maintaining its electric transmission lines. The ordinance is necessary due to the Seattle City Charter's requirement that all acquisition and disposal of real property interests must be approved by the City Council, as well as a state law requiring that land originally acquired for public utility purposes be sold at fair market value. The sale is expected to generate $452,216 in revenue for the City's Light Fund in 2026. The legislation has been reviewed by City Light engineers, who have determined that there is no conflict between the road project and the electric transmission corridor. The ordinance would also decrease the need for City Light maintenance along the corridor, resulting in cost savings. A public hearing is required for this legislation, and maps showing the location of the property are attached. If the ordinance is not implemented, it would impede the completion of Snohomish County's road improvement project and delay City Light's receipt of just compensation for the property.

title: legislation-7958605-supporting-Summary Att 1 - Snohomish County 43rd Ave SE Summary Maps
baseline_headline: Snohomish County 43rd Ave SE Road Improvement Project
baseline_body: The legislative text appears to be a summary of a road improvement project in Snohomish County, specifically focusing on 43rd Ave SE. The project involves several phases of construction, including the provision of a new roundabout connection intersection, widening of the roadway in Phase 1, and intersection improvements in Phase 2. The project area is bounded by several landmarks, including North Creek High School, Fernwood Elementary, and intersections with 180th St SE, 184th St SE, 187th St SE, 188th St SE, and 196th St SE. The project also references a UGA (Urban Growth Area) boundary, suggesting that the improvements are intended to support growth and development in the area. The text is accompanied by summary maps, which are likely used to illustrate the project's scope and location. Overall, the project aims to improve the safety and efficiency of the roadway, likely to accommodate increasing traffic volumes and support local economic growth.

title: legislation-7958605-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Property Transfer to Snohomish County for Road Improvement Project
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council has passed an ordinance allowing Seattle City Light to transfer a portion of its real property interests to Snohomish County. The transfer, which includes approximately 62,400 square feet of property, is in support of Snohomish County's road-widening project on 43rd Ave SE/Sunset Rd. The project aims to improve 1.7 miles of the 43rd Ave Corridor, enhancing safety and promoting multi-modal mobility throughout the area. In exchange for the property transfer, Snohomish County will make a one-time payment of $452,216, representing the fair market value of the property rights. The ordinance ensures that Seattle City Light's property rights necessary for the safe and reliable transmission of electricity are preserved, while also supporting the needs of a partner jurisdiction. The transfer is authorized under the Seattle City Charter, which grants the City Council powers to acquire or dispose of real property on behalf of the city government, both within and outside city limits. The project is expected to increase the functionality and connection of the county's road system, serving as a minor collector roadway that funnels traffic from local roads to the arterial network.

title: legislation-7958605-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Approves Sale of City Light Property to Snohomish County for Road Widening
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance authorizing the sale of a portion of the City Light Department's real property to Snohomish County for road widening purposes. The property, located along 43rd Avenue SE in Snohomish County, is part of City Light's electric transmission corridor. Snohomish County has offered to purchase the property for $452,216, which is the fair market value. The sale is possible because the City Light Department has determined that it no longer needs the property for its utility purposes and has declared it surplus to its needs. As part of the sale, the City Light Department will reserve an easement for electric transmission line purposes, allowing it to continue accessing and maintaining its transmission lines. The General Manager and Chief Executive Officer of the City Light Department is authorized to execute a Quit Claim Deed and accept payment from Snohomish County, which will be deposited into the Light Fund. The ordinance takes effect in accordance with the Seattle Municipal Code, and the sale is expected to facilitate the widening of 43rd Avenue SE to accommodate growing traffic demands in the area.

title: legislation-7963779-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council to Approve Payment of Bills for Week of March 16-20, 2026
baseline_body: The Office of City Finance has introduced an ordinance to authorize the payment of certain claims for the week of March 16, 2026, through March 20, 2026. The legislation requires City Council approval, as mandated by RCW 42.24.180, to pay financial obligations for bills and payroll expenses paid out of the PeopleSoft system. The payment of these claims does not have a direct impact on the City's budget, as the funds were previously appropriated by the City Council. The ordinance authorizes the payment of valid claims, including bills and payroll expenses, for all City departments. If the legislation is not implemented, the City may face greater legal and financial liability for not paying its legal obligations. The payment of these claims is a routine process, and the legislation does not require a public hearing, notice to be published in The Daily Journal of Commerce or The Seattle Times, or affect a specific piece of property. Additionally, the legislation does not have any significant impacts on vulnerable or historically disadvantaged communities, climate change, or create a new program with long-term, measurable goals. Overall, the ordinance is a standard procedure to ensure the City's financial obligations are met in a timely manner.

title: legislation-7963779-full
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Ordinance for Payment of Claims and Expenditures
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance approving the payment of various claims and expenditures for the week of March 16, 2026, through March 20, 2026. The total amount approved for payment is $96,899,915.81, which includes $23,052,393.93 in mechanical warrants, $56,009.56 in e-payables, and $73,837,512.32 in electronic financial transactions. The ordinance ensures that these payments are made in accordance with the remaining appropriations in the current budget, as amended, and in compliance with relevant state laws, including RCW 42.24.180 and RCW 35.32A.090(1). The ordinance also ratifies and confirms any prior acts taken consistent with its authority, prior to its effective date. The ordinance was passed by the City Council and signed by the Mayor on March 31, 2026, and will take effect as provided by the Seattle Municipal Code. The approval of this ordinance demonstrates the city's commitment to managing its finances and ensuring that all expenditures are made in a lawful and transparent manner.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Executive Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes 2026 Library Levy to Enhance Community Resources and Services
baseline_body:
The Seattle Public Library has proposed a 2026 Library Levy, titled "Read. Grow. Belong," which aims to invest in various aspects of the library system to promote literacy, community engagement, and empowerment. The levy proposal is based on Mayor Wilson's vision, which emphasizes the importance of libraries in providing valuable resources and services to the community. The proposed levy will invest in several key areas, including books and materials, public spaces, early literacy programs, enrichment materials and programs for all ages, and empowerment classes and resources.

The development of the levy proposal involved extensive community engagement, including community conversations, surveys, and workshops with partners and stakeholders. The proposal outlines specific investment areas, including hours and access, building maintenance, technology and online services, collections, and administration. The levy will provide funding for open hours at 27 libraries, early learning support, all-ages programs, and capital improvements, among other initiatives.

The proposed 2026 Library Levy will be a 7-year property tax levy, with a total funding amount of $410 million, which is an increase from the 2019 levy of $219.1 million. The cost to taxpayers will be approximately $163 per year for residential property owners, based on the median assessed residential value. The levy will be used to sustain and expand existing services, including maintaining open hours, early literacy programs, and security personnel, as well as adding new funding for initiatives such as more all-ages programs, increased security and emergency preparedness, and the establishment of an Office of Inclusion and Belonging.

The proposal highlights the value of the library to the community, citing a return on investment of $16.48 for every $1 spent in 2025, and a value of nearly $8.4 million provided to small business entrepreneurs in 2025. The levy proposal aims to build on this value and provide enhanced resources and services to the community, aligning with Mayor Wilson's vision of a city where everyone can read, grow, and belong.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Central Staff Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Property Tax Overview: Breakdown of Tax Levies, Rates, and Revenue
baseline_body:
The provided legislative text offers an in-depth analysis of property taxes in Seattle, including the various types of tax levies, their revenue contributions, and the calculations involved in determining tax rates. According to the 2026 adopted budget, property taxes account for 17% of the total $5.3 billion in non-utility revenues, with $915 million coming from property tax levies. This revenue is split between the General Fund, which receives $327 million (16% of total revenues), and other funds. The text explains key terms such as tax levy, lid lift, assessed value, levy rate, and levy capacity, which are essential for understanding the property tax system.

The document categorizes property tax levies into four types: General Purpose Levy, Voter-Approved Levies for Specific Purposes (such as Move Seattle and Libraries for All), Levy by Special Taxing Districts (like the Seattle Metropolitan Parks District), and Excess Levy to Pay Debt Service on Voter-Approved Bonds. Each type has distinct characteristics, such as growth limits, voter approval requirements, and rate caps. The combined levy rate for General Purpose and Voter-Approved Levies is limited to $3.60 per $1,000 of assessed value, while the overall local government limit, including county and special district levies, is $5.90 per $1,000.

The text also provides formulas for calculating the property tax rate for the city and the tax amount for individual properties, using the total assessed value and the levy collection amount. An example calculation is given for the Move Seattle Levy, demonstrating how to determine the tax rate and the annual property tax amount for a median assessed home value of $833,000. The breakdown of the 2026 tax payment for a median AV home shows the distribution of taxes across various levies, including the City General Fund, Library Services, and Transportation Levy Rate. Overall, the document offers a comprehensive overview of Seattle's property tax system, highlighting its complexities and the various factors that influence tax rates and revenue.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Central Staff Memo
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes $410 Million Library Levy for 2027-2033 to Enhance Public Library Services
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is considering a new library levy, Council Bill (CB) 121181, which would replace the expiring 2019 Library Levy and provide approximately $410 million for the Seattle Public Library (SPL) services over seven years, from 2027 through 2033. The proposed levy would raise the property tax rate to $0.19 per $1,000 in assessed value in 2027, up from $0.12 per $1,000 in 2026, with an average rate of $0.16 per $1,000 over the seven-year period. The levy would provide about $58.6 million annually to support SPL, representing a 55% increase from the 2019 Library Levy. The funds would be allocated to five categories of library services, including continued services ($339.2 million) and new, added services ($70.8 million). The levy would require a simple majority (50% plus 1) of voters to pass and would be exempt for seniors, veterans with disabilities, and other persons with disabilities who qualify. The City must file the ordinance with King County Elections by May 1 to request placement on the August 4, 2026, ballot. The proposed levy aims to enhance library services, including the construction of new branch libraries, renovation of existing branches, and development of new programs and services. If approved, the levy would provide significant funding for SPL, allowing the library to expand its services and improve its facilities over the next seven years.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Proposed Amendment Packet
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Amendments to 2026 Library Levy Ordinance
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Select Committee on the Library Levy has proposed several amendments to the 2026 Library Levy Ordinance (CB 121181). The amendments aim to enhance the services and infrastructure of the Seattle Public Library. One of the key amendments, sponsored by Councilmember Foster, proposes to increase the levy amount by $10 million to fund major renovations at the Central Library, including improvements to the Children's Area and the Seattle Room. This amendment would increase the maximum annual levy and expected aggregate levy amount over seven years. Other proposed amendments include funding for multilingual play and learn programs, community resource specialists, cooling and deferred maintenance, seismic retrofit of historic branch libraries, electronic materials and collections staffing, e-book peak picks, library programming on the Seattle Channel, physical accessibility improvements, and safety ambassadors. The amendments also aim to promote equity, expand access to education and opportunity, and invest in libraries as community landmarks. The proposed levy would raise approximately $420 million over seven years, with excess funds available for other city purposes. The City Council intends to place the levy proposal on the August 4, 2026, primary election ballot to replace the expiring levy and continue funding library programs and operations for the next seven years.

title: legislation-7958606-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Expands Lease Authority for Transitional Encampments to Address Homelessness
baseline_body:
The proposed ordinance aims to expand the Director of Finance and Administrative Services' authority to execute leases for land used for transitional encampment purposes, a key step towards achieving Mayor Katie B. Wilson's goal of opening 1000 new shelter and emergency housing beds in 2026. Currently, the Director is prohibited from signing lease agreements for land exceeding 18,000 square feet, with capped rental costs per square foot. This legislation increases the square foot limit to 65,000 and allows market-rate rental costs, enabling the City to secure larger sites and reducing the timeframe for standing up microshelter villages from 7-12 months to 3-4 months.

The ordinance is part of a package of three bills designed to advance the Mayor's goal. The other two companion bills increase the census limit for transitional encampments to 150 people per site citywide, with one site allowed up to 250 people per council district, and amend the 2026 Budget to make available resources for capital and operating costs associated with standing up transitional encampment programs. The proposed changes will utilize unappropriated fund balances from the Downtown Health and Human Service Account and the Low-Income Housing Fund, sourced from Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) funds.

By expanding the lease signing authority and increasing the census limit, the City aims to efficiently use limited City-owned lands, maximize the number of persons served on each site, and increase the speed with which tiny house villages can grow. This legislation is expected to help address the City's homelessness crisis by providing more shelter and emergency housing options, and streamlining the process for securing and developing transitional encampment sites.

title: legislation-7958606-supporting-Central Staff Memo (CBs 121184 & 121185)
baseline_headline: City Council Considers Three Bills to Increase Shelter Units and Address Homelessness
baseline_body: The City Council is currently reviewing three proposed Council Bills (CBs) aimed at increasing shelter units and addressing homelessness in the city. The bills are part of the Executive's strategy to add 1,000 new shelter units by 2026, with a goal of opening 500 units by June 1, 2026, before the World Cup games begin. The first bill, which will be introduced to the Land Use and Sustainability Committee, would increase the census count for transitional encampments, allowing for more people to be accommodated in these facilities. The second bill, CB 121184, would enable the Director of the Department of Finance and Administrative Services to execute leases for larger sites to be used as transitional encampments, eliminating the maximum lease limit. The third bill, CB 121185, would allocate $4.9 million in unused funding to support the development of new shelters. The bills are designed to support the expansion of micro-modular shelters, also known as tiny home villages, which have been shown to be a desirable form of shelter due to their privacy, security, and ability to accommodate couples, families, and pets. The city currently funds 16 micro-modular shelters, providing 621 shelter units, with an average utilization rate of 86%. The proposed legislation aims to increase the number and size of these shelters, providing more supportive services and accommodations for those in need. The bills will be discussed in committee meetings, with potential votes scheduled for April 7, and are expected to play a crucial role in the city's efforts to address homelessness and provide adequate shelter for its residents.

title: legislation-7958606-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes Legislation to Expedite Shelter Expansion for Homeless Population
baseline_body: The proposed 2026 Shelter Expansion legislation, Council Bill (CB) 121184, aims to accelerate the creation of 1,000 new units of shelter and emergency housing in Seattle by streamlining the process of selecting and preparing sites for shelter programs. The current process is plagued by delays, with service providers facing challenges in navigating permits, negotiating lease terms, and finding suitable land. The proposed legislation would grant the Finance & Administration Services (FAS) Director greater flexibility in signing lease agreements, increasing the square footage limit from 18,000 to 65,000 square feet and removing the cost per square foot cap to allow market costs. This would enable the city to quickly select and prepare sites, which could then be turned over to shelter operators, significantly expediting the opening of new microshelter programs. The legislation is expected to reduce the site selection and preparation process from 9-12 months to 3-5 months, allowing the city to provide shelter to more people in need. The proposed process would also shift the responsibility of site preparation from service providers to the city, streamlining the process and enabling providers to focus on operating the shelters. Overall, the legislation aims to address the urgent need for shelter expansion in Seattle, particularly with the city expecting a busy summer season and the FIFA 2026 event.

title: legislation-7958606-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Expands Authority for Leases of Transitional Encampments
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance that amends the Seattle Municipal Code to expand the authority of the Director of Finance and Administrative Services to execute leases for transitional encampment purposes. The ordinance modifies Section 3.127.020 of the Seattle Municipal Code, which governs the execution of leases for use by City Departments and agencies. The changes allow for more flexible leasing terms when the land is used for transitional encampments, increasing the maximum square footage that can be leased in a single rental agreement from 18,000 to 65,000 square feet. The price per square foot for such leases will be negotiated consistent with market rates. The ordinance also retains existing provisions that cap rental payments for office space, improved space, and unimproved real estate, with annual adjustments based on the Consumer Price Index. Additionally, the ordinance ratifies and confirms any prior acts taken consistent with its authority, and it will take effect as provided by the Seattle Municipal Code. The changes aim to support the city's efforts to provide temporary housing solutions for individuals in need, while also ensuring that leasing arrangements are fiscally responsible and aligned with market rates.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Considers Legislation to Expand Transitional Encampments and Increase Shelter Beds for Homeless Individuals
baseline_body:
The proposed legislation aims to support the Mayor's goal of opening 1000 new shelter and emergency housing beds in 2026 by amending the 2026 Budget to allocate resources for capital and operating costs associated with transitional encampment programs. The bill affects two fund sources: the Downtown Health and Human Service Account and the Low-Income Housing Fund, which is sourced from Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) funds. The unappropriated fund balance in the Downtown Health and Human Service Account will be used to benefit downtown Seattle and support new programs for unsheltered individuals, while the Low-Income Housing Fund will be utilized for capital costs to establish more shelter. 

This legislation is part of a package of three bills that work together to advance the goal of increasing shelter beds. The companion legislation includes increasing the census limit for transitional encampments from 100 people per site to 150 people per site citywide, with one site allowed up to 250 people per council district. This change aims to efficiently utilize limited City-owned lands and expand existing microshelter villages. Additionally, the legislation proposes expanding the Director of Finance and Administrative Services' lease signing authority for land used for transitional encampments, allowing for market-rate rental costs and increasing the square foot limit from 18,000 to 65,000 square feet. This change is intended to accelerate the process of securing sites and establishing transitional encampments, which currently can take seven to twelve months. By passing this legislation, the City Council aims to provide more shelter and support services to homeless individuals and make progress towards addressing the city's homelessness crisis.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Central Staff Memo (CBs 121184 & 121185)
baseline_headline: City Council Considers Three Bills to Increase Shelter Units and Address Homelessness
baseline_body: The City Council is considering three proposed Council Bills (CBs) aimed at increasing shelter units and addressing homelessness in the city. The bills are part of the Executive's strategy to add 1,000 new shelter units by 2026, with a goal of standing up 500 units by June 1, 2026, before the World Cup games begin. The first bill, which will be introduced to the Land Use and Sustainability Committee, would increase the census count for transitional encampments, allowing for more people to be accommodated in these facilities. The second bill, CB 121184, would enable the Director of the Department of Finance and Administrative Services to execute leases for larger sites to be used as transitional encampments, eliminating the maximum lease limit. The third bill, CB 121185, would allocate $4.9 million in unused funding for new shelters. The bills are intended to support an increase in micro-modular shelters, also known as tiny home villages, which provide private and secure accommodations for individuals and families. The city currently funds 16 micro-modular shelters, operated by various providers, including the Low-Income Housing Institute and Catholic Community Services, offering a total of 621 shelter units. The average utilization rate for these shelters is 86%, indicating a high demand for this type of accommodation. The proposed bills aim to address the city's homelessness crisis by providing more shelter units, increasing the capacity of existing facilities, and allocating funding for new shelters. The City Council will review and discuss the bills in the coming weeks, with potential votes scheduled for April 7.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: 2026 Shelter Expansion Council Bill Allocates $17.55 Million for Homelessness Services
baseline_body:
The 2026 Shelter Expansion Council Bill (CB 121185) aims to address homelessness by allocating $17.55 million for the expansion of shelter services in the region. The estimated costs for the 2026 shelter expansion include one-time start-up costs of $9 million and ongoing costs of $8.5 million for the first six months, with total estimated costs of $17.55 million. The ongoing annual costs for 2027 and beyond are estimated to be $17 million. The cost per unit varies by program type, ranging from $25,000 to $45,000. The bill authorizes $8.22 million in funding, with $4.9 million available for immediate spending and an additional $3.3 million requiring a two-step appropriation process to transfer funds from the Housing Fund to the Human Services Fund.

The funding sources identified for the shelter expansion include $3.3 million from the Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Office of Housing (OH) Revolving Loan Fund Conversion, $2.8 million from CDBG-COVID funds, $1.6 million from the Downtown Fund, and $2.1 million from Proviso HSD-060-A-1. Additionally, $7.75 million will be used from the King County Regional Homelessness Authority (KCRHA) 2025 General Fund Underspend. The CDBG-COVID funds must be spent by July 2026 and have a nexus with COVID prevention, while the Downtown Fund and Proviso Funds are subject to specific use requirements. The KCRHA Underspend of $7.7 million will be used for shelter expansion, following the completion of outstanding invoices and work. The bill requires Council approval for the allocation of these funds. Overall, the 2026 Shelter Expansion Council Bill aims to provide critical funding for addressing homelessness in the region.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Proposed Amendments to CB 121185
baseline_headline: Proposed Amendments to CB 121185 Aim to Enhance Homeless Shelter Services and Management
baseline_body: The proposed amendments to CB 121185, which allocates $4.9 million for new shelters, aim to improve the management and effectiveness of homeless shelter services. The amendments, sponsored by various committee members, include requirements for collaboration between the Executive and the committee to maintain existing shelters and create new ones, while addressing the structural budget gap. Additionally, the Mayor's Office is expected to submit a report on the implementation of the first 500 shelter units and a plan to achieve 4,000 new units overall by September 14, 2026. Other amendments focus on prioritizing shelter units for people experiencing unsheltered homelessness in the surrounding neighborhood, establishing zones around shelters to prohibit unsanctioned encampments, and developing public safety plans to maintain safe and clean areas around new shelters. Specifically, at least one new shelter should be dedicated to people in recovery from substance abuse disorders, and at least one-third of new shelter units should be reserved for people experiencing homelessness in the same neighborhood as the shelter. These amendments aim to enhance the overall management and provision of homeless shelter services, ensuring that they are safe, effective, and responsive to the needs of the local community.

title: legislation-7958607-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Allocates Additional $8.2 Million to Address Homelessness
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance aimed at enhancing its response to homelessness by increasing appropriations to various departments and budget control levels. The ordinance amends the 2026 Budget, which was adopted through Ordinance 127362, and includes changes to the 2026-2031 Capital Improvement Program (CIP). A total of $8,221,868 is being allocated to address homelessness, with the funds being distributed as follows: $1,589,440 to the Human Services Department from the Downtown Health and Human Service Account - General Trust Fund, $3,316,214 to the Finance General department from the Low-Income Housing Fund, and $3,316,214 to the Human Services Department from the Human Services Fund. These funds will be used to cover necessary costs and expenses incurred in 2026 that were not initially accounted for in the budget due to unforeseen circumstances. The ordinance also ratifies and confirms any prior acts consistent with its authority and will take effect in accordance with the Seattle Municipal Code. The passage of this ordinance demonstrates the city's commitment to addressing the issue of homelessness and providing essential services to those in need.

title: legislation-7971874-attachment-Att 2 - Slope Stabilization Easement (Duchin)
baseline_headline: King County Slope Stabilization Easement Agreement Outlines City's Rights and Responsibilities
baseline_body: The Slope Stabilization Easement agreement between the City of Seattle and a property owner (Grantor) in King County, Washington, establishes the terms and conditions for the City's use of a designated easement area on the property. The agreement prohibits the installation of utility facilities within five horizontal feet of the easement area and grants the City the right to enter the property to perform work related to slope stabilization, without prior notice in emergency situations. The City is responsible for restoring any damaged private improvements to their original condition and is liable for any claims, costs, or damages arising from its actions on the property, unless the damage is caused by the property owner. The property owner retains the right to use the property for any legal purpose not inconsistent with the easement agreement and can request relinquishment of the easement if the property is redeveloped in a way that eliminates the need for drainage. The City must exercise its rights under the agreement in accordance with applicable statutes, orders, rules, and regulations, and will defend and indemnify the property owner against any claims or damages arising from the City's actions. The agreement also includes provisions for the City's liability and indemnification obligations, as well as the process for relinquishing the easement if it is no longer needed.

title: legislation-7971874-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance to Accept Slope Stabilization Easements for NE 91st Street Project
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance aims to authorize the General Manager and CEO of Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to accept permanent slope stabilization easements on two private properties located at 3822 NE 91st Street and 3832 NE 91st Street. The easements are necessary for the NE 91st Street Culvert and Slope Stabilization Project, which aims to stabilize a steep slope that has shown signs of instability and partial failure of a culvert conveying street runoff to Maple Creek. The project involves the construction of a Reinforced Soil Slope (RSS) to ensure long-term stability, and the easements will allow SPU to construct and maintain the necessary infrastructure on a limited part of the private properties. The cost of acquiring the easements is $9,900, which is included in SPU's current budget. The ordinance also ratifies and confirms certain prior acts related to the project. If the legislation is not implemented, SPU may face increased maintenance and emergency repair costs, and reduced drainage system performance. The project is expected to have no additional financial impacts beyond the initial cost, and periodic maintenance will be funded through existing operations and maintenance resources.

title: legislation-7971874-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Considers Ordinance for Slope Easement Acquisition to Stabilize NE 91st Street Culvert
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Government & Utilities Committee is considering an ordinance that authorizes Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to accept two permanent slope easements on private properties located at 3822 and 3832 NE 91st Street in the Wedgwood Neighborhood. The easements are necessary to ensure long-term access for drainage and slope stabilization as part of the NE 91st St Culvert and Slope Stabilization Project. The project aims to address the instability of the slope and the failure of the culvert, which was first identified in 2022 and temporarily mitigated with the installation of a temporary culvert in 2023. The permanent repairs will involve the construction of a reinforced soil slope and new drainage infrastructure. The two permanent slope easements will be acquired for a combined fair market value of $9,900, and the agreements have been reached through amicable negotiations with the affected property owners. The SPU has engaged with the community throughout the design process and plans to maintain ongoing communication during construction. The ordinance is intended to provide a permanent solution to the slope instability and culvert failure, ensuring the long-term safety and functionality of the drainage system in the area.

title: legislation-7971874-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Authorizes Acceptance of Slope Stabilization Easements for Stormwater Management
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance authorizing the General Manager and CEO of Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to accept slope stabilization easements on two private properties located at 3822 NE 91st Street and 3832 NE 91st Street. The easements, granted by property owners Michael and Elizabeth Van Snellenberg and Linda Duchin, will allow SPU to install, operate, maintain, repair, and replace stormwater drainage and slope stabilization infrastructure on the properties. The ordinance is aimed at addressing drainage issues at the end of NE 91st Street, which could affect roadway stability and cause erosion to public property. The property owners have agreed to the terms and fair market value compensation for the easements, and the City Council has approved the ordinance. The real property rights and interests conveyed by the easements will be placed under the jurisdiction of SPU, and any prior acts consistent with the authority of the ordinance are ratified and confirmed. The ordinance will take effect as provided by the Seattle Municipal Code, and it is intended to prioritize maintaining drainage within natural drainage courses and controlling surface water runoff on steep slopes and landslide-prone areas.

title: legislation-7971875-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Public Utilities Seeks to Acquire Land for Wastewater Pump Station 4 Replacement Project
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance aims to authorize Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to acquire two parcels of land, located at South Director Street in the Rainier Beach neighborhood, through negotiation or eminent domain. The acquired land will be used for the Wastewater Pump Station 4 (PS 4) Replacement Project, which seeks to build a new, permanent pump station to handle projected sewer flows from anticipated redevelopment in the area. The existing PS 4 lacks adequate capacity, and the replacement project is crucial to maintaining SPU's service level for the sewer basin. The two parcels are being sold together for $760,000, which will be absorbed into the existing SPU budget. The project budget has sufficient funds set aside for property acquisition, and the costs will not require additional funding or have significant financial impacts on the City. The acquisition of the property is essential for the project to move forward, and failure to do so may result in pump station failure and sanitary sewer overflows. The legislation does not require a public hearing but necessitates publication of notice in the Daily Journal of Commerce and the Seattle Times. The project's impact on vulnerable or historically disadvantaged communities is not explicitly stated, but it is expected to maintain SPU's service level and prevent potential environmental hazards in the Rainier Beach neighborhood.
