title: legislation-7971875-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Considers Legislation to Acquire Properties for Pump Station 4 Replacement Project
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Governance and Utilities Committee is considering legislation to authorize the Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to acquire two properties in Council District #2, necessary for the replacement of the existing Pump Station 4. The current pump station is aging and deteriorating, requiring replacement to manage future wastewater flows associated with development in the area. The proposed project involves purchasing the two properties, located near a shipping container facility and a Verizon facility, for a total of $760,000, with each property costing $380,000. The funding for the acquisition will come from SPU's Drainage and Wastewater Rates. The parcel owners are willing sellers, and the acquisition is considered the most cost-effective option for replacing the existing pump station. The new pump station will be larger, providing the capacity to support future development in the area. The preliminary schedule for the project includes completing design work by Q2 2029, starting construction in Q3 2029, and finishing construction by Q3 2031. The legislation aims to ensure the timely replacement of the pump station, which is critical for managing wastewater flows and supporting future growth in the area.

title: legislation-7971875-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Authorizes Acquisition of Properties for Pump Station 4 Replacement Project
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance authorizing the acquisition of two parcels of land, identified as King County Parcel Numbers 4265700090 and 4265700085, located at South Director Street, for the purpose of replacing an existing pump station as part of the Pump Station 4 Replacement Project. The ordinance grants the General Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Seattle Public Utilities, or their designee, the authority to negotiate and acquire the necessary real property interests, including deeds, easements, and temporary construction easements, through negotiation or eminent domain (condemnation) if necessary. The acquisition will be funded from allocated funds, and the City will provide just compensation to the property owners. The ordinance also authorizes the City Attorney to commence and prosecute condemnation proceedings, and to stipulate for the purpose of minimizing damages. The acquired properties will be used for utility and general municipal purposes, and will be placed under the jurisdiction of Seattle Public Utilities. The ordinance ratifies and confirms prior acts taken in connection with the project, and authorizes the General Manager and Chief Executive Officer to execute all necessary documents to complete the acquisition. The project aims to resolve current and future issues with the existing pump station, and the acquired properties are necessary to complete the project.

title: legislation-7964173-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note vD3
baseline_headline: Seattle Updates Stormwater Code to Enhance Environmental Protection and Compliance
baseline_body: The Seattle Public Utilities department has proposed an ordinance to update the 2026 Stormwater Code, which aims to protect life, property, public health, and the environment from the adverse impacts of urban stormwater runoff. The revised code includes updates to stormwater control requirements for development, roadways, utilities, and maintenance activities, as well as strengthened stormwater treatment and infiltration standards. The changes are intended to ensure the City's compliance with the State Department of Ecology's requirements, particularly the 2024-Phase I Municipal Stormwater Permit (MS4 Permit). The updates will incorporate new Ecology requirements, policy changes, and improvements to usability, with an effective date of July 1, 2026. The legislation will have financial implications for the City, including capital and operations and maintenance costs, as well as the need for additional training for staff in several departments. The updates will impact various City departments, including Seattle Public Utilities, Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections, Seattle Department of Transportation, Seattle Parks and Recreation, Seattle City Light, and the Finance and Administrative Services department. The proposed changes are designed to reduce the risks of flooding, water pollution, landslides, and erosion, and to promote environmentally critical areas and best available science practices.

title: legislation-7964173-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note vD2
baseline_headline: Seattle Updates Stormwater Code to Enhance Environmental Protection and Compliance
baseline_body: The Seattle Public Utilities department has proposed an ordinance to update the city's Stormwater Code, which aims to protect life, property, public health, and the environment from the adverse impacts of urban stormwater runoff. The revised code strengthens stormwater treatment and infiltration standards, clarifies vesting and review thresholds, and ensures the city's compliance with the requirements of the State Department of Ecology. The updates include new requirements for development, roadways, utilities, and maintenance activities, as well as revisions to the associated Director's Rule (Stormwater Manual). The changes are necessary to comply with the city's 2024-Phase I Municipal Stormwater Permit, which was effective on August 1, 2024. The updated code will incorporate new Ecology requirements, policy changes, and improvements to usability. The ordinance will amend several chapters of the Seattle Municipal Code and will have financial implications for the city, including costs associated with training staff and implementing the new code. The effective date of the updated code is July 1, 2026. The proposal includes a new Director's Rule and updates to the Stormwater Manual, which will provide guidance on the implementation of the revised code. Overall, the updated Stormwater Code aims to enhance environmental protection, reduce the risks of flooding, water pollution, landslides, and erosion, and ensure the city's compliance with state and federal regulations.

title: legislation-7964173-supporting-Summary Ex A - Legislative Summary
baseline_headline: Seattle Updates Stormwater Code to Comply with State and Federal Regulations
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is updating its Stormwater Code and associated Stormwater Manual to comply with the 2024-2029 Phase I Municipal Separate Storm Sewer System (MS4) Permit issued by the Washington State Department of Ecology. The updates aim to maintain the city's compliance with state and federal water quality permits, while also incorporating independent city policy and usability improvements based on the best available science. The revised code and manual will align with recent land use code updates, modernize technical standards, and support city priorities related to urban greening, resilience, and water quality. Key changes include updated definitions, thresholds, and requirements to match the permit and the Stormwater Management Manual for Western Washington, as well as revised exemptions, clarified vesting dates, and modified roadway project thresholds. The updates will affect administration, source control, development, and construction site stormwater pollution prevention control, and are necessary to maintain the city's permit coverage. The revised code and manual must be adopted and take effect by July 1, 2026, to meet the permit requirements. The updates will provide improved clarity and predictability for applicants and reviewers, and will help protect life, property, public health, and the environment from the adverse impacts of urban stormwater runoff, including flooding, water pollution, landslides, and erosion.

title: legislation-7964173-supporting-Summary Ex B - Ecology Conditional Approval Letter
baseline_headline: Washington State Department of Ecology Grants Preliminary Approval to Seattle's Stormwater Manual and Municipal Code
baseline_body: The Washington State Department of Ecology (Ecology) has completed its review of the City of Seattle's updated Stormwater Manual and Municipal Code, which were effective as of July 1, 2021. The review aimed to determine if the updated documents are equivalent to the required portions of Ecology's 2024 Stormwater Management Manual for Western Washington (SWMMWW). The preliminary determination is that the reviewed items are equivalent, with some exceptions that require further communication before they can be deemed equivalent. The exceptions include specific tables and sections related to stormwater management, such as the definition of Common Plan of Development or Sale, closely related projects, and certain best management practices (BMPs). The City of Seattle is required to adopt and make the revised program effective by July 1, 2026. If changes are made to the listed items during the adoption process, they will need further review by Ecology. The final determination will be made after a public review process associated with a future modification of the Phase I Permit. The letter serves as a response to the City of Seattle's submission and outlines the next steps in the approval process. Ecology appreciates the City's efforts to develop revised stormwater management regulations and rules, and the department is available to answer any questions or concerns.

title: legislation-7964173-supporting-Summary Ex C - Environmentally Critical Areas
baseline_headline: Seattle Updates Stormwater Code to Incorporate Best Available Science and Comply with State Regulations
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is revising its Stormwater Code to comply with the requirements of the 2024-2029 Phase I Municipal Stormwater Permit (MS4 Permit) issued by the Washington State Department of Ecology. The revised code aims to incorporate the best available science in developing policies and regulations to protect the functions and values of critical areas, as required by the Revised Code of Washington (RCW) 36.70A.172 and the Washington Administrative Code (WAC) 365-195-900 through WAC 365-195-925. The report provides a compilation and review of selected literature on urban stormwater management, focusing on the impacts of stormwater runoff on receiving waters, including changes in flow rates and volumes, and water quality. The review also examines best management practices (BMPs) for stormwater management. The updated code and associated Stormwater Manual will include minimum requirements, thresholds, definitions, and other specified requirements, limitations, and criteria, as determined by the Department of Ecology. The report builds upon previous best available science reviews, including the 2015 and 2021 reviews, and supplements the City's Environmentally Critical Areas: Best Available Science Reviews, which provide detailed reviews of the best available science regarding wetlands, fish and wildlife conservation areas, geologic hazard areas, flood-prone areas, abandoned landfills, and critical aquifer recharge areas. The revised Stormwater Code and Stormwater Manual will improve the City's stormwater management practices, ensuring that they are at least as protective of facility function and functionally equivalent to the Department of Ecology's Stormwater Management Manual for Western Washington.

title: legislation-7964173-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Updates Stormwater Code to Meet Regulatory Requirements and Improve Environmental Protection
baseline_body: The Seattle Public Utilities is updating the Stormwater Code to comply with the Department of Ecology's Stormwater Permit and align with current city priorities. The code applies to private development, city projects, businesses, and residents, and is administered by various city departments. The update aims to protect people, property, and the environment from stormwater runoff damage, and includes requirements for source control, construction site pollution prevention, and onsite stormwater management. The majority of the updates are technical in nature and required by the Department of Ecology, with minimal fiscal impacts to the city and capital projects. The city has conducted extensive public outreach and received over 550 comments, with many incorporated into the updated code. The updated code is scheduled to be adopted by the full council in May and will become effective on July 1, 2026. Key objectives of the update include regulatory compliance, policy alignment, and improved clarity and usability. The city has also hosted listening sessions and meetings with key partners, development communities, and the general public to discuss concerns and gather feedback. Overall, the updated Stormwater Code aims to improve environmental protection, water quality, and tree preservation, while also supporting the city's obligations to comply with state and federal regulations.

title: legislation-7964173-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Updates Stormwater Code to Enhance Water Quality and Compliance with State Regulations
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance to update its Stormwater Code, effective in 2026, with the primary goal of strengthening stormwater treatment and infiltration standards to protect local water bodies, including creeks, lakes, the Duwamish River, and Puget Sound. The updates aim to reduce pollutants and contaminants from stormwater runoff, such as 6PPD-q, a chemical in tire dust that harms local salmon populations. The ordinance is designed to balance environmental protection with the need for increased development to address urgent housing needs. Key amendments include expanding opportunities for tree planting to achieve the City's goal of 30% tree canopy coverage by 2037 and clarifying vesting and review thresholds. The updates also ensure the City's compliance with the requirements of the 2024-2029 Phase 1 Municipal Stormwater Permit issued by the State Department of Ecology, which mandates minimum requirements for stormwater management in new development and redevelopment projects. The ordinance amends several chapters of the Seattle Municipal Code, including Chapters 22.800, 22.801, 22.802, 22.803, 22.805, and 22.807, to reflect the City's commitment to protecting the environment and public health while promoting sustainable development. The State Department of Ecology has reviewed and approved the proposed revisions, finding them to be in compliance with the MS4 Permit requirements. Overall, the 2026 Stormwater Code Update is a crucial step towards enhancing water quality, reducing stormwater runoff pollutants, and promoting a healthier environment in Seattle.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Updated Proposed Amendments Packet (added; 3/31/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Amendments to 2026 Library Levy Ordinance
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Select Committee on the Library Levy has proposed several amendments to the 2026 Library Levy Ordinance (CB 121181). The amendments aim to enhance the services and programs offered by the Seattle Public Library, with a focus on equity, accessibility, and community engagement. The proposed amendments include funding for multilingual play and learn programs, community resource specialists, cooling and deferred maintenance, seismic retrofitting of historic branch libraries, and electronic materials and collections staffing. Additionally, amendments propose improvements to physical accessibility, digital materials, and safety ambassadors. Two specific amendments, sponsored by Councilmember Saka, focus on digital skilling programs for teens at the South Park and High Point Branch Libraries, and contracting with the Washington State Black Legacy Institute for programming and local history curation that celebrates the history of Washington state's Black communities. The amendments would allocate a portion of the Library levy funding amount, as transmitted by the Mayor, to support these initiatives. The proposed Library levy funding amount includes $38.2 million for technology services and $218.4 million for operating hours and access, with increases in funding for all-ages programs, security, and emergency preparedness. The City of Seattle intends to place a levy proposal on the August 4, 2026, primary election ballot to replace the expiring levy and continue providing financial support to the Library's programs and operations for the next seven years.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Proposed Amendment Packet (4/8/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Amendments to 2026 Library Levy Ordinance
baseline_body:
The Seattle City Council is considering a series of amendments to the proposed 2026 Library Levy Ordinance, CB 121181. The amendments aim to enhance the city's library services and facilities over the next seven years. One of the key amendments, sponsored by Councilmember Foster, proposes to increase the levy amount by $10 million to fund major renovations at the Central Library, including improvements to the Children's Area and the Seattle Room. The amendment would also increase the maximum annual levy and expected aggregate levy amount in Section 2 of the ordinance.

Other proposed amendments include funding for multilingual play and learn programs, community resource specialists, cooling and electrification of branch libraries, seismic retrofitting of historic branch libraries, and improvements to physical accessibility and elevators. Additionally, amendments propose funding for digital skilling programs for teens, electronic and physical materials and collections staffing, and programming on the Seattle Channel.

The proposed levy would provide $85.9 million for maintenance, including $28.9 million more than ongoing levy support, and would raise approximately $420 million in aggregate over seven years for library services. The amendments would need to be approved by the City Council and then submitted to voters for approval in the August 4, 2026, primary election. If approved, the levy would replace the expiring 2019 Library Levy and provide continued financial support to the city's library programs and operations. The amendments aim to promote equity, expand access to education and opportunity, and invest in libraries as treasured landmarks and spaces for learning and discovery.

title: legislation-7972725-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Ordinance to Pay Outstanding Bills and Payroll for Week of March 23-27, 2026
baseline_body: The Office of City Finance has introduced an ordinance to authorize the payment of certain claims for the week of March 23, 2026, through March 27, 2026. The legislation requires City Council approval, as mandated by RCW 42.24.180, to pay outstanding bills and payroll expenses. The payment of these claims will be made from funds that were previously appropriated by the City Council, and therefore, the passage of this bill will not have a direct impact on the City's budget. The ordinance covers all financial payment obligations for bills and payroll paid out of the City's financial system, PeopleSoft. If the City fails to pay its legal obligations, it could face greater legal and financial liability. The legislation affects all City departments, as it authorizes the payment of bill and payroll expenses for all departments. The ordinance does not require a public hearing, notice to be published in The Daily Journal of Commerce or The Seattle Times, or affect a specific piece of property. Additionally, the legislation does not have any significant impacts on vulnerable or historically disadvantaged communities, climate change, or create a new program with long-term, measurable goals. Overall, the ordinance is a routine measure to ensure the City's financial obligations are met in a timely manner.

title: legislation-7972725-full
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Ordinance for Payment of Claims and Payroll Expenses
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance authorizing the payment of various claims and payroll expenses for the week of March 23, 2026, through March 27, 2026. The ordinance approves the payment of a total of $25,050,174.18 for claims, $91,074.84 for e-payables, and $68,377,755.36 for electronic financial transactions. Additionally, it authorizes the payment of $72,673,797.82 for gross payrolls, including mechanical warrants, manual warrants, agencies warrants, and direct deposits. The ordinance ensures that these payments are made within the remaining appropriations in the current budget as amended, in accordance with relevant state laws and municipal codes. The ordinance also ratifies and confirms any prior acts taken consistent with its authority, and it will take effect as provided by the Seattle Municipal Code. The City Council passed the ordinance on April 7, 2026, and it was signed by the Mayor and attested by the City Clerk.

title: legislation-7964172-attachment-Att 1 - Agreement By and Between City of Seattle and Local 32
baseline_headline: City of Seattle and Local 32 Reach Collective Bargaining Agreement for 2025-2027
baseline_body: The City of Seattle and the United Association of Journeymen and Apprentices of the Plumbing and Pipe Fitting Industry, Local 32, have entered into a collective bargaining agreement effective from January 1, 2025, to December 31, 2027. The agreement outlines the terms and conditions of employment for City employees represented by Local 32, including wages, hours, benefits, and working conditions. The agreement consists of 28 articles and three appendices, covering a range of topics such as non-discrimination, union membership and dues, grievance procedures, work stoppages, classifications and rates of pay, vacations, holidays, leaves, retirement, health care, and safety standards. The agreement also establishes labor-management committees, outlines the role of union representatives, and sets forth the rights and responsibilities of both the City and the Union. Additionally, the agreement addresses issues such as probationary periods, hours of work and overtime, transfers, layoffs, and recalls, as well as discipline and productivity standards. The agreement is intended to promote a positive and productive working relationship between the City and Local 32, and to provide a fair and equitable framework for the employment of City employees represented by the Union. Overall, the agreement aims to balance the interests of both parties and to ensure a stable and efficient workforce for the City of Seattle.

title: legislation-7964172-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Collective Bargaining Agreement with Local 32 Plumbers and Pipefitters Union
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council has authorized the execution of a collective bargaining agreement (CBA) between the City of Seattle and the United Association of Journeymen and Apprentices of the Plumbing and Pipe Fitting Industry, Local 32. The three-year agreement, which covers approximately 150 City employees, outlines wages, benefits, hours, and working conditions from January 1, 2025, to December 31, 2027. The agreement includes significant wage adjustments, ranging from 4.0% to 11.0% in 2025, and annual wage increases of 4.0% in 2025, 3.6% in 2026, and a CPI-based increase in 2027. Additionally, the agreement provides for increased vacation days, bereavement leave, and a premium increase for Water Pipe Worker Series employees with certain certifications. The footwear allowance will also be increased to $350 in 2025 and $375 in 2026 and 2027. The financial implications of the agreement will be borne mostly by Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) ratepayers, and separate legislation will be introduced to authorize additional appropriations for City departments. The agreement replaces the previous CBA, which expired on December 31, 2024, and aims to improve working conditions and compensation for Local 32 members employed by the City of Seattle.

title: legislation-7964172-supporting-Summary Att 1 - Bill Draft Agreement By and Between City of Seattle and Local 32
baseline_headline: City of Seattle and Local 32 Reach Collective Bargaining Agreement
baseline_body: The City of Seattle and the United Association of Journeymen and Apprentices of the Plumbing and Pipe Fitting Industry, Local 32, have entered into a collective bargaining agreement effective January 1, 2025, through December 31, 2024. The agreement outlines the terms and conditions of employment for City employees represented by Local 32, including wages, hours, benefits, and working conditions. The agreement is divided into 28 articles, covering topics such as non-discrimination, recognition, labor-management committees, union membership and dues, grievance procedures, work stoppages, classifications and rates of pay, vacations, holidays, leaves, retirement, health care, and safety standards. The agreement also addresses issues related to union representatives, work outside of classification, probationary periods, hours of work and overtime, transfers, layoffs, and recalls. Additionally, the agreement includes provisions related to apprenticeship, productivity, discipline, and the rights of management. The agreement is intended to promote a positive and productive working relationship between the City and Local 32, and to provide a framework for resolving disputes and addressing employee concerns. The agreement includes appendices A, B, and C, which provide additional details and specifications related to the terms and conditions of employment. Overall, the agreement aims to ensure fair treatment, competitive compensation, and a safe working environment for City employees represented by Local 32.

title: legislation-7964172-full
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Collective Bargaining Agreement with Local 32 Plumbers and Pipefitters Union
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance authorizing the execution of a collective bargaining agreement between the city and the United Association of Journeymen and Apprentices of the Plumbing and Pipe Fitting Industry, Local 32. The previous agreement expired on December 31, 2024, but employees continued to work while negotiations for a new agreement were underway. The newly approved agreement, attached to the ordinance as Attachment 1, outlines the terms and conditions of employment, including wages, hours, benefits, and other conditions. The ordinance authorizes the Mayor to execute the agreement on behalf of the city. Additionally, the ordinance ratifies and confirms any actions taken prior to its effective date that are consistent with the agreement. The city's Budget Office will introduce separate legislation to provide budget appropriation authority to cover compensation items authorized in the agreement. The ordinance will take effect in accordance with the Seattle Municipal Code, and it has been approved by the City Council and signed by the Mayor, with the City Clerk responsible for filing the document.

title: legislation-7964174-attachment-Att A - 2026 SLIs by Council Committee
legislation-7944140-supporting-Presentation (4/1/26)
baseline_headline: Skagit River Hydroelectric Project Relicensing Agreement Moves Forward with New Settlement
baseline_body: The Skagit River Hydroelectric Project has reached a significant milestone with the adoption of an ordinance authorizing the Mayor to sign a Comprehensive Settlement Agreement, marking a crucial step towards obtaining a new 50-year license. The project's original 50-year license, issued in 1927, expired on April 30, 2025, and has been operating on annual licenses since then. The new settlement agreement is the result of a multi-year process that began in 2018, involving studies, license applications, and settlement discussions. The agreement outlines the next steps, including submission to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) in May 2026, followed by a public comment period, environmental assessment, and consultations with various agencies. The anticipated timeline for the new license issuance is 2030. The agreement also outlines early actions, including the establishment of a Skagit License Implementation Committee, ecosystem monitoring, cultural resource protection, and flow and habitat planning. Additionally, the agreement addresses operations and water quality, fish passage programs, and other environmental and conservation measures. The City Council will be requested to pass an ordinance to accept the new license once it is issued. The settlement agreement aims to balance the project's hydroelectric operations with environmental and conservation goals, ensuring the long-term sustainability of the Skagit River ecosystem.

title: legislation-7958606-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes Legislation to Accelerate Shelter Expansion with Increased Flexibility in Lease Agreements
baseline_body: The proposed 2026 Shelter Expansion legislation, Council Bill (CB) 121184, aims to accelerate the creation of 1,000 new units of shelter and emergency housing in Seattle by streamlining the process of selecting and preparing sites for shelter programs. The legislation would grant the Finance & Administration Services (FAS) Director greater flexibility in signing lease agreements, increasing the square footage limit from 18,000 to 65,000 square feet and removing the cost per square foot cap to allow market costs. This would enable the city to quickly select and prepare sites, reducing the delay time from 7-9 months to 3-5 months, and expedite the opening of new microshelter programs. The proposed process would shift site preparation and selection from service providers to the city, allowing providers to focus on operating the shelters. The legislation is intended to address the challenges faced by service providers, including navigating permits, negotiating lease terms, and finding suitable land, and to provide a safe place indoors for more people during a period of high demand, including the busy summer season and the FIFA 2026 event.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: 2026 Shelter Expansion Council Bill Allocates $17.55 Million for Homelessness Services
baseline_body: The 2026 Shelter Expansion Council Bill (CB 121185) aims to address homelessness by allocating $17.55 million for the expansion of shelter services in the region. The estimated costs for the expansion include one-time start-up costs of $9 million and ongoing costs of $8.5 million for the first six months, with an estimated annual cost of $17 million for 2027 and beyond. The cost per unit is estimated to be between $25,000 and $45,000, varying by program type. The bill authorizes the allocation of funds from various sources, including the Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) Office of Housing Revolving Loan Fund Conversion, CDBG-COVID funds, Downtown Fund, Proviso HSD-060-A-1, and the King County Regional Homelessness Authority (KCRHA) 2025 General Fund Underspend. The CDBG-COVID funds, which expire in July 2026, must be spent first and have a nexus with COVID prevention. The Downtown Fund, established in 1985, must be used for downtown health and human services, while the Proviso HSD-060-A-1 funds can be used for substance use disorder treatment and other behavioral health supports. The KCRHA 2025 Underspend of $7.7 million will also be used for shelter expansion. The bill requires a two-step appropriation process to transfer funds from the Housing Fund to the Human Services Fund, with a total of $8.22 million authorized for appropriation. The remaining funds will be allocated through a separate process, subject to Council approval. Overall, the 2026 Shelter Expansion Council Bill aims to provide critical funding for homelessness services and support the expansion of shelter beds in the region.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Initial Proposed Amendments to CB 121185 (3/30/26)
baseline_headline: Proposed Amendments to Bill CB 121185 Aim to Enhance Homeless Shelter Services and Management
baseline_body: The proposed amendments to Bill CB 121185, which allocates $4.9 million for new shelters, aim to improve the management and effectiveness of homeless shelter services. The amendments, sponsored by various committee members, include requirements for collaboration between the Executive and the committee to maintain existing services and create new shelter units while addressing the structural budget gap. The Mayor's Office is expected to submit a report by September 14, 2026, detailing the implementation of the first 500 shelter units and a plan to achieve the goal of 4,000 new units. Additionally, the amendments propose that at least one new shelter should be designated for individuals recovering from substance abuse disorders, providing a clean and sober environment. Priority for shelter units should be given to people experiencing unsheltered homelessness in the neighborhood and district where the shelter is located. The Executive is also expected to establish and enforce zones around each shelter to prevent unsanctioned encampments. Furthermore, a public safety plan should be developed and implemented to keep the area surrounding new shelters safe, clean, and free of encampments. The amendments also suggest that at least one-third of newly created shelter units should be reserved for people experiencing homelessness in the neighborhood where a new shelter is located, to the extent possible. Overall, the proposed amendments aim to enhance the effectiveness and management of homeless shelter services, prioritize the needs of local communities, and ensure a safe and clean environment for both shelter residents and surrounding neighborhoods.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-Proposed Amendments to CB 121185 (4/7/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Amendments to Transitional Encampment Expansion Budget Ordinance
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council's Finance, Native Communities and Tribal Governments Committee is considering a series of amendments to the Transitional Encampment Expansion Budget Ordinance (CB 121185). The proposed amendments aim to address various aspects of the city's efforts to expand shelter units and services for individuals experiencing homelessness. The amendments include requirements for the Mayor's Office to develop and implement a public safety plan, provide monthly reports on public safety indicators, and collaborate with the Council to determine the acuity level and services to be provided for new shelter units. Additionally, the amendments propose that the Human Services Department prioritize shelter and services providers with expertise in working with disproportionately impacted populations, operate at least one shelter for people in recovery from substance abuse disorder, and open at least two new shelters for women and children. The amendments also emphasize the importance of maintaining existing Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH) and addressing the structural budget gap. One of the amendments, sponsored by Councilmember Kettle, requests a report from the Mayor's Office on the implementation of the first 500 shelter units and a plan to achieve 4,000 new units overall, including information on location, services offered, and operating costs. The amendments are intended to ensure that the city's efforts to expand shelter units and services are effective, efficient, and responsive to the needs of individuals experiencing homelessness.

title: legislation-7964170-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Accepts Non-City Funding and Amends 2026 Budget
baseline_body: The proposed ordinance allows the Mayor or their designee to accept and authorize the expenditure of grants, private funding, and subsidized loans from non-City sources. The legislation aims to update the 2026 Budget and the 2026-2031 Capital Improvement Program (CIP) to reflect the acceptance of these funds. The ordinance includes requests from various City departments to accept grants, donations, and other private funding agreements, which will modify the Adopted Budget for the first quarter of 2026. The financial implications of the ordinance include an expenditure change of $4,491,193 from the General Fund and $32,457,051 from Other Funds in 2026, with corresponding revenue changes. The ordinance does not create or delete any positions, and the costs associated with the legislation can be absorbed within existing operations. The ordinance also includes a provision to automatically abandon automatic carryforward items between $0.00 and $1.00 without returning to Council, which will assist departments in cleaning up small remaining appropriation authority. Overall, the ordinance seeks to formalize the acceptance of non-City funding and ensure that the City's budget and CIP are updated to reflect these changes.

title: legislation-7964170-supporting-Summary Att A - 2026 Q1 Acceptance Ordinance Detail Table
baseline_headline: City Accepts Grants Totaling Over $10.7 Million for Energy Efficiency, Community Response, and Affordable Housing Initiatives
baseline_body: The city has accepted several grants totaling over $10.7 million to support various initiatives, including energy efficiency, community response, and affordable housing. The grants are from the Association of Washington Cities and the Washington State Department of Commerce. The largest grant, worth $9,950,000, is for the Connecting Housing to Infrastructure (CHIP) program, which will support utility infrastructure improvements for the Fort Lawton project, allowing for the development of affordable housing on the site. Another grant of $176,000 will fund energy audits at 8 City-owned buildings to advance compliance with the State's Clean Building Performance Standards (CBPS). The city has also accepted a grant of $100,000 for the Community Assisted Response and Engagement (CARE) program, which provides alternative behavioral and mental health first response for individuals in crisis. Additionally, the city has accepted two grants totaling $612,203 from the Washington State Home Energy Assistance Program-Weatherization (SHEAP-Wx) to provide low-income households with adequate heating and cooling systems. All of the grants have a limited period of performance, ranging from 7/1/2025 to 6/30/2027, and do not require a local match or incur new ongoing costs for the city. The grants will be staffed by existing city staff, including the Office of Sustainability and Environment and the Office of Housing. Overall, these grants will support the city's efforts to improve energy efficiency, provide community response services, and increase access to affordable housing.

title: legislation-7964170-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle City Budget Office Proposes $37 Million in Revenue Acceptance for Various City Departments
baseline_body: The City Budget Office (CBO) of Seattle has proposed a revenue acceptance ordinance for the first quarter of 2026, covering the period from September 2025 to April 2026. The ordinance accepts and appropriates approximately $4.5 million from the General Fund and $32.5 million from other funds, totaling $37 million, to be allocated across four City departments. The funding will support various initiatives, including $6.4 million for supportive services for older adults, $2 million for a shared-use path construction, and $5 million for environmental remediation efforts. Additionally, the ordinance includes funding for climate change mitigation efforts, such as city building energy audits and transit public outreach. The proposed ordinance consists of 34 items, with all grant match requirements met by existing appropriations. The funding will be distributed to several departments, including the Human Services Department (HSD), Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT), Seattle Public Utilities (SPU), and the Finance and Administrative Services (FAS) department, to support various projects and programs. The ordinance is subject to approval by the Finance, Native Communities, and Tribal Government Committee.

title: legislation-7964170-supporting-Central Staff Memo
baseline_headline: City Council to Discuss $40.8 Million Grant Acceptance and Appropriation Ordinance
baseline_body: The Finance, Native Communities & Tribal Governments Committee will discuss Council Bill 121187, which authorizes City departments to accept and appropriate $40.8 million in grant revenues from non-City sources. The bill is the first comprehensive grant acceptance and appropriation legislation of 2026, with a total appropriation of $37 million. The grants will be used to fund various projects and services across several City departments, including the Office of Housing, Human Services Department, Seattle Department of Transportation, Seattle Fire Department, Seattle Police Department, and Seattle Public Utilities. Some of the notable grants include a $10 million grant for affordable housing development, a $6.4 million grant for supportive services for older adults, and a $5 million grant for remediation efforts at the Lower Duwamish Waterway Superfund site. The bill also requires a total of $7.1 million in local grant matches, which will be met through various City funds, including the Drainage and Wastewater Fund and the 2024 Transportation Levy. The grants will support a range of initiatives, including utility infrastructure improvements, care coordination for older adults, transportation upgrades, and environmental remediation. The Committee's discussion of the bill is the next step in the process, and if approved, the ordinance will provide the necessary authority for City departments to accept and expend the grant funds.

title: legislation-7964170-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Authorizes Acceptance of Non-City Funding for Various Projects and Initiatives
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance authorizing the Mayor or their designee to accept and expend funding from non-City sources, including grants, private funding, and subsidized loans. The ordinance amends the 2026 Budget and the 2026-2031 Capital Improvement Program (CIP) to reflect the acceptance of these funds. The funding will support various projects and initiatives across different departments, including energy audits, community crisis response, affordable housing development, low-income household weatherization, supportive services for older adults, transportation improvements, and transit enhancements. Specifically, the ordinance authorizes the acceptance of funds from organizations such as the Association of Washington Cities, the Washington State Department of Commerce, King County, Sound Transit, and the Washington State Department of Social and Health Services. The total amount of funding authorized is approximately $20.5 million, which will be deposited into various funds, including the General Fund, Low Income Housing Fund, Human Services Fund, and Transportation Fund. The ordinance also revises project allocations for certain projects in the CIP and ratifies and confirms certain prior acts. Overall, the ordinance aims to leverage non-City funding to support key initiatives and projects that align with the City's goals and priorities.

title: legislation-7980173-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes Expansion of Transitional Encampments to Address Homelessness Crisis
baseline_body: The proposed legislation aims to increase the capacity of transitional encampments in Seattle to provide temporary shelter for the growing homeless population. The bill would raise the census limit for transitional encampments to 150 people per site citywide, with one site allowed up to 250 people per council district, exceeding the current limit of 100 people per site. This change is intended to efficiently utilize limited City-owned lands, particularly when smaller microshelter villages already exist on the sites and can be expanded. The legislation is part of a package of three bills introduced to achieve Mayor Katie B. Wilson's goal of opening 1,000 new shelter and emergency housing beds in 2026. By increasing the number of occupants in each transitional encampment, the City hopes to accelerate the production of temporary shelter, especially in light of recent federal funding actions that penalize local jurisdictions using a "housing first" approach. The proposal waives the requirement for a pre-adoption public hearing and is exempt from review under the State Environmental Policy Act, with a post-adoption public hearing to be held within 60 days of adoption. The legislation is introduced as interim, with a one-year duration, during which the Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections will conduct a study to inform the development of permanent legislation. The companion legislation includes expanding the Director of Finance and Administrative Services' lease signing authority for land used for transitional encampments, allowing rental costs at market rate, and increasing the square foot limit for lease agreements. If passed, the legislation would help address the urgent need for temporary shelter and accelerate the production of housing units, ultimately supporting the City's efforts to mitigate the homelessness crisis.

title: legislation-7980173-supporting-Mayor's Office Presentation (4/15/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle Council Bill 121195 Aims to Increase Shelter Capacity with Revised Census Limits
baseline_body:
Council Bill 121195, proposed by the Land Use and Sustainability Committee, seeks to increase the census limits for transitional encampments in Seattle. The current land use code limits the number of people per site to 100, resulting in unused public land while demand for shelter remains high. The bill proposes to increase the citywide census limit for transitional encampments with micro-modular shelters or RV safe lots from 100 to 150 people, with one site allowed to accommodate up to 250 people. This legislation is interim and will expire after 12 months, during which time the Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections (SDCI) will conduct a State Environmental Policy Act (SEPA) analysis and develop permanent regulations to be transmitted in Winter 2027.

The bill is supported by examples of successful large-scale microshelter programs in other cities, such as Tampa, Florida, and Los Angeles, California, which have demonstrated that these programs can operate effectively and serve a large number of people. The proposed legislation also emphasizes the importance of community partnership, public safety, and neighborhood coordination, with requirements for Good Neighbor Agreements, 24/7 staffing, and defined site boundaries. The Seattle Police Department and Seattle Fire Department will work with operators to ensure site safety and review site plans before construction. The goal of the bill is to provide more shelter options for those in need while maintaining safe and welcoming neighborhoods through collaboration and shared responsibility.

title: legislation-7980173-supporting-Central Staff Presentation (4/15/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Advances Legislation to Increase Transitional Encampment Shelters
baseline_body:
The Seattle City Council is moving forward with legislation aimed at increasing the capacity of transitional encampment shelters in the city. The proposed bills, CB 121184, CB 121185, and CB 121195, are designed to provide more shelter options for individuals in need. CB 121184 authorizes the execution of leases for large sites to accommodate larger transitional encampments, while CB 121185 increases funding for new shelter in transitional encampments. CB 121195, which is still under discussion, would increase the number of allowed occupants in transitional encampments on a temporary basis and establish a work program to develop ongoing changes to regulations for transitional encampments.

The legislative intent for the transitional shelter budget increase includes several key provisions, such as requiring the Human Services Department to report on implementation by September 14, 2026, with a financial plan for providing 4,000 new shelter units. The Executive is also being asked to develop a public safety plan for transitional shelter, with monthly reporting on public safety indicators. Additionally, the legislation calls for the establishment of a shelter acuity work group to advise on services for encampments used as shelter, and for providers with expertise in culturally appropriate services to be utilized.

The proposed legislation also includes specific requirements for transitional shelters, such as providing at least one shelter for people in recovery from substance abuse disorders and at least two shelters serving families with children. The Council is also prioritizing permanent supportive housing while increasing transitional shelter capacity. The next steps in the process include continued discussion of CB 121195 on April 29, with a possible vote on May 6, and a full Council vote on May 12. Overall, the legislation aims to provide more shelter options and supportive services for individuals in need, while also prioritizing public safety and culturally appropriate services.

title: legislation-7980173-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Aims to Expand Transitional Encampments to Address Homelessness Crisis
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance to expand the capacity of transitional encampments, also known as "tiny house villages" or microshelters, as a temporary solution to address the worsening homelessness crisis. The ordinance amends Sections 23.42.054 and 23.42.056 of the Seattle Municipal Code to accommodate this expansion. The City Council has declared that the homelessness crisis, which was first declared a civil emergency in 2015, continues to worsen, with a 260% increase in homelessness since 2014 and a 150% increase in deaths among people presumed to be homeless. The ordinance recognizes the importance of permanent supportive housing as a long-term solution but acknowledges that it takes time and substantial funds to develop. In the interim, the City is focusing on expanding transitional encampments, which have shown promise as a relatively low-cost and acceptable solution for people experiencing homelessness. The ordinance is part of the City's comprehensive approach to addressing homelessness, which includes a "housing-first" strategy that prioritizes providing permanent housing to individuals, even if they are still addressing underlying issues such as mental health or addiction. The City's efforts to develop additional microshelter villages have taken on increased urgency due to recent actions by the federal government, and the ordinance aims to support this effort by providing interim provisions to expand the capacity of transitional encampments.

title: legislation-7980174-attachment-Full Text: CB 121196 v1
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Aims to Increase Housing Supply and Affordability through Zoning Amendments
baseline_body:
The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance to address the ongoing housing affordability challenge by increasing the housing supply through zoning amendments. The ordinance proposes to rezone certain areas in the University District, Fremont neighborhood, Madison/Miller neighborhood, Rainier Beach neighborhood, and Downtown neighborhood to support multi-purpose redevelopment, remove code barriers to sustainable construction methods, and incentivize community-serving uses. The amendments aim to facilitate the construction of more housing, including affordable housing, by allowing for the conversion of commercial spaces to residential use, promoting upper-level housing above non-profit owned community facilities, and supporting innovative building practices such as mass timber, modular, and passive house construction.

The ordinance is designed to align with the One Seattle Comprehensive Plan, which aims to increase housing supply through broad citywide zoning changes. The proposed amendments will apply the City's Mandatory Housing Affordability (MHA) requirement to the rezoned areas, ensuring that new developments include affordable housing units. The ordinance also takes into account the need to avoid development pressure on historic landmarks, sites with character structures, and displacement risk areas.

The specific changes include amendments to various sections of the Seattle Municipal Code, including Sections 23.40.080, 23.47A.009, 23.49.008, 23.49.011, 23.49.036, 23.49.058, and 23.49.158, as well as the addition of a new Section 23.40.090 and a new Map 1L to Chapter 23.49. The ordinance is intended to provide a targeted approach to increasing housing supply in focused opportunity areas, while also promoting sustainable and community-oriented development practices. Overall, the ordinance aims to contribute to a more affordable and vibrant housing market in Seattle.

title: legislation-7980174-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Zoning Amendments to Increase Housing Supply and Affordability
baseline_body:
The Office of Planning and Community Development has introduced legislation aimed at increasing Seattle's housing supply and affordability through zoning amendments. The proposed changes target specific areas, including the University District, Fremont neighborhood, Madison/Miller neighborhood, Rainier Beach neighborhood, and Downtown neighborhood. The legislation consists of seven key components: (1) rezoning the southern end of the Stone Way corridor to allow for infill housing opportunities, (2) expanding the Downtown Retail Core to incentivize adaptive reuse of underutilized office buildings for housing, (3) rezoning areas with nonprofit and public entity ownership to facilitate redevelopment, (4) removing code barriers to passive house, modular, and mass timber construction, (5) increasing height limits for residential uses in Belltown, (6) amending development standards to support multi-purpose redevelopment, and (7) incentivizing community-serving uses along with housing construction.

The proposed amendments aim to generate an additional 3,000-4,000 new homes in the next decade, with a focus on affordable housing through the application of the City's Mandatory Housing Affordability (MHA) requirements. The changes are designed to support near-term investment in housing supply, while broader changes are implemented through the Seattle Comprehensive Plan update. The legislation also responds to state legislation (2SHB 1183) by removing façade modulation and upper-level setback requirements for buildings constructed using innovative methods, such as passive house, modular, or mass timber construction. Overall, the proposed zoning amendments aim to increase housing production, choice, and affordability in Seattle, while minimizing displacement pressure in targeted areas.

title: legislation-7980174-supporting-Director's Report
baseline_headline: Seattle Proposes Zoning Amendments to Boost Housing Supply and Affordability
baseline_body: The City of Seattle is introducing a legislative package aimed at increasing the city's housing supply and affordability through zoning amendments and land use code changes. The proposed legislation, comprised of seven components, seeks to encourage near-term investment in housing development, particularly in areas with unique advantages for new housing production without causing displacement pressure. The key components of the legislation include: (1) rezoning land in the Fremont/Stone Way corridor to unlock infill housing production near major employers; (2) expanding the Downtown Retail Core rezone to allow residential towers and incentivize the conversion of underused historic office structures to housing; (3) supporting multi-purpose redevelopment on sites with community-based uses, such as public or nonprofit-owned sites, to combine revitalized community facilities with increased housing; (4) removing code barriers to passive house, modular, and mass timber construction; (5) increasing residential height limits and modifying development standards in Belltown to encourage more housing; (6) removing code barriers to converting commercial space to housing in more zones; and (7) incentivizing grocery store retention and housing in Lake City. The proposed changes would be subject to the city's Mandatory Housing Affordability (MHA) requirements, ensuring that affordable housing is included in new developments. The legislation aims to spur new housing developments in the next one to three years, contributing to the city's goal of dramatically expanding housing production, choice, and affordability.

title: legislation-7980174-supporting-Presentation (4/15/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle Introduces Housing Opportunities Legislation to Boost Affordable Housing Supply
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced the Housing Opportunities Legislation, aimed at making focused changes to the city's land use code and zoning to support near-term investment in additional housing supply. The legislation has several key components, including rezone proposals for areas such as Fremont/Stone Way, Expanded Downtown Retail Core, and Belltown, which aim to encourage more housing development without causing displacement pressure. The legislation also seeks to remove code barriers to innovative construction methods such as passive house, modular, and mass timber construction, as implemented by HB 1183. Additionally, the legislation proposes to increase height limits and modify development standards in certain areas, such as Belltown, to encourage more housing development. The legislation also includes incentives for grocery store retention and housing in Lake City, where developers can benefit from increased height limits and floor area ratio (FAR) increments if they include certain community uses and residential dwelling units in their developments. The overall goal of the legislation is to advance housing production on opportunity sites and in areas that are well-positioned to spur new housing development, while broader changes to implement the new Comprehensive Plan are being worked on. The legislation is expected to support the development of more affordable housing units in Seattle, with examples of potential projects including a 17-story hybrid mass timber tower in the First Hill neighborhood.

title: legislation-7980174-full
baseline_headline: Seattle Ordinance Aims to Increase Housing Supply and Promote Sustainable Development
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has introduced an ordinance that seeks to increase the city's housing supply and promote sustainable development through various zoning and land use changes. The ordinance proposes to rezone certain areas in the University District, Fremont neighborhood, Madison/Miller neighborhood, Rainier Beach neighborhood, and Downtown neighborhood to allow for more housing and community-based uses. The changes aim to support multi-purpose redevelopment on specific sites, removing code barriers to innovative construction methods such as passive house, modular, and mass timber construction. Additionally, the ordinance seeks to incentivize the conversion of commercial spaces to housing in more zones and encourage community-serving uses alongside new housing developments. To achieve these goals, the ordinance amends several sections of the Seattle Municipal Code, including Sections 23.40.080, 23.47A.009, 23.49.008, 23.49.011, 23.49.036, 23.49.058, and 23.49.158, and adds a new Section 23.40.090. The ordinance also updates the Official Land Use Map, adding a new Map 1L to Chapter 23.49 and amending Chapter 23.32 at various pages. Overall, the ordinance aims to promote more sustainable, equitable, and community-focused development in Seattle, addressing the city's housing needs while supporting innovative and environmentally friendly construction practices.

title: legislation-7980806-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Ordinance to Pay Outstanding Bills and Payroll Expenses
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council has introduced an ordinance to appropriate funds for paying certain claims, including bills and payroll expenses, for the week of March 30, 2026, through April 3, 2026. The ordinance, prepared by the Office of City Finance, authorizes the payment of previously appropriated funds, which will not have a direct impact on the city's budget. The legislation is required by RCW 42.24.180, which mandates that the City Council authorize the payment of certain claims. The ordinance covers all financial payment obligations for bills and payroll paid out of the city's financial system, PeopleSoft. The passage of this bill is necessary to ensure the city meets its legal obligations and avoids potential legal and financial liability. The legislation does not create or amend a Capital Improvement Program (CIP) project, and it does not have any significant financial implications or costs that would require shifting resources away from other work. The ordinance is a routine administrative measure to ensure the city's financial obligations are met, and it affects all city departments by authorizing the payment of their bills and payroll expenses.

title: legislation-7980806-full
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Approves Ordinance for Payment of Claims and Expenditures
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has passed an ordinance appropriating funds to pay certain claims for the week of March 30, 2026, through April 3, 2026. The total amount approved for payment is $113,858,473.80, which includes $19,648,475.88 in mechanical warrants, $154,553.34 in e-payables, and $94,055,444.58 in electronic financial transactions. The ordinance is consistent with the remaining appropriations in the current budget, as amended, and complies with the relevant state law (RCW 42.24.180) and municipal code. The ordinance also ratifies and confirms any prior acts taken consistent with its authority, ensuring that all expenditures are valid and authorized. The ordinance was passed by the City Council and signed by the Mayor on April 14, 2026, and will take effect in accordance with the Seattle Municipal Code. The approval of this ordinance allows the city to settle outstanding claims and complete financial transactions, maintaining the city's financial operations and obligations.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Updated Table of Contents for Proposed Amendment Packet (3/31/26)
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Amendments to 2026 Library Levy Ordinance
baseline_body: The Select Committee on the Library Levy has proposed a series of amendments to the Seattle Public Library 2026 Levy Ordinance, known as CB 121181. The proposed amendments aim to enhance and expand various services and facilities offered by the Seattle Public Library. The amendments include provisions for multilingual play and learn programs, as well as English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) programs, sponsored by Council Members Foster and Juarez. Other proposed amendments focus on improving community resources, such as hiring Community Resource Specialists and providing badge access, also sponsored by Council Member Foster. Additionally, the amendments address infrastructure and maintenance needs, including cooling and deferred maintenance, seismic retrofitting of historic branch libraries, and improving physical accessibility and elevator services. The proposals also emphasize the importance of digital resources, including electronic materials and collections staffing, e-book services, and digital skilling programs for teens. Furthermore, the amendments suggest partnering with external organizations, such as the Washington State Black Legacy Institute, and restoring the West Seattle Branch Library. Other proposals include increasing safety measures through safety ambassadors or contracted outreach services and promoting library programming on the Seattle Channel. The proposed amendments are intended to enhance the overall quality and accessibility of library services for the Seattle community.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-April 8 Amendment Vote Tally
baseline_headline: Seattle Public Libraries 2026 Levy Ordinance Amendments Receive Committee Approval
baseline_body: The Select Committee on the Library Levy voted on several amendments to the proposed 2026 levy ordinance for the Seattle Public Libraries (CB 121181) on April 8, 2026. A total of 13 amendments were considered, with 13 amendments passing and none failing. The approved amendments cover a range of topics, including: multilingual play and learn programs, community resource specialists, cooling and electrification of library branches, seismic retrofitting of historic library buildings, electronic and physical materials collections, e-book services, library programming on the Seattle Channel, physical accessibility improvements, digital skilling for teens, and support for the Washington State Black Legacy Institute. The amendments were sponsored by various committee members, including Foster, Lin, Rinck, Strauss, and Saka. The vote tally shows that most committee members voted in favor of the amendments, with some members abstaining or not present for certain votes. The passage of these amendments suggests that the committee is prioritizing initiatives that promote diversity, equity, and accessibility in the Seattle Public Libraries, as well as investing in the maintenance and upgrading of library facilities and services.

title: legislation-7952576-supporting-Amendment A
baseline_headline: Seattle City Council Proposes Technical Amendment to Library Levy Ordinance
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council has proposed a technical amendment to the Seattle Public Libraries 2026 Levy Ordinance, sponsored by Councilmember Rivera. The amendment, known as Amendment A Version 1 to CB 121181, aims to correct the dollar amount shown in the definition of "Library levy funding amount" in Section 1.D of the ordinance. The correction would change the amount from $410,000,000 to $479,760,000, which is consistent with the changes made to the ordinance by the Select Committee on the Library Levy on April 8, 2026. This amendment is intended to ensure consistency within the ordinance and does not make any substantive changes to the library levy funding amount. The proposed amendment would update the definition of "Library levy funding amount" to reflect the increased levy amount over seven years resulting from the proposition's passage. The correction is necessary to maintain accuracy and consistency in the ordinance, and the amendment is expected to be adopted to finalize the library levy ordinance.

title: legislation-7958607-supporting-April 4 Amendment Vote Tally
baseline_headline: City Council Approves Amendments to Expand Transitional Encampments and Improve Homelessness Services
baseline_body: The Finance, Native Communities and Tribal Government committee has approved several amendments to the MO Transitional Encampment Expansion – Budget Ordinance (CB 121185). The amendments aim to improve the city's response to homelessness by expanding shelter units, enhancing public safety, and providing targeted services to vulnerable populations. Key provisions include: developing a public safety plan to keep areas surrounding new shelters safe and clean; requiring quarterly reports on public safety indicators; convening a shelter and acuity workgroup to determine the level of care and services needed; prioritizing shelter units for people experiencing homelessness in the same neighborhood; operating at least one shelter for people in recovery from substance abuse disorder; and opening at least one new shelter for families with children. Additionally, the committee affirmed its commitment to maintaining existing Permanent Supportive Housing and collaborating with the Executive to address the structural budget gap. All amendments were approved with a "yes" vote, with some committee members absent or not present. The approved amendments will inform the implementation of the first 500 shelter units and the overall goal of achieving 4,000 new units.

title: legislation-7971875-supporting-Certificate of Mailing
baseline_headline: City of Seattle Initiates Condemnation Proceedings for Property Acquisition
baseline_body: The City of Seattle has initiated condemnation proceedings to acquire a property located at Parcel Number 426570-0085, owned by Sharon L. Olsson, as part of the Seattle Public Utilities Pump Station 4 Replacement project. A notice of condemnation was mailed to the property owner on March 17, 2026, and published in the Seattle Times and the Daily Journal of Commerce on March 23 and 30, 2026. The notice informs the property owner that the City Council is expected to vote on an ordinance authorizing the acquisition of the property through negotiation or condemnation. The proposed ordinance will be presented to the Parks, Public Utilities & Technology Committee on April 9, 2026, and the property owner has been invited to attend the meeting and provide comments on the proposed legislation. The City is required to provide formal written notice to the property owner at least 15 days before taking final action authorizing condemnation, as mandated by RCW 8.25.290. The property in question is located in the Rainier Beach area of Seattle and is described in the attached exhibit as LOT 15, LENHART & LINDSAY'S RAINIER BEACH TRACTS. The City's Real Property Services department is handling the condemnation proceedings, and the property owner can contact Bryan Solemsaas, Sr. Real Property Agent, for further information.
